candidates.evidence: done.store(true, Ordering::Release) runs on the main thread right after the spawn loop (line 365), concurrently with all worker threads that still hold round-robin-seeded items in their deques (push at line 297). A worker that empties its own deque (worker.pop()==None) and gets only Steal::Empty/Steal::Retry from sibling stealers then hits `if done.load(...) { break; }` (line 329) and exits while other deques still contain unprocessed WorkItems it could have stolen. Each abandoned WorkItem is an input section whose bytes (sec_buf.copy_from_slice) and relocations (apply_reloc) are never written, so those section ranges stay zeroed in the output. peony-emit has NO tests (grep found no `mod tests`/`#[test]`), so the failing path is uncovered and cargo test green is not evidence of correctness.

candidates.summary: Steal::Retry treated identically to Steal::Empty, so transient CAS contention is mistaken for no-work, accelerating premature worker exit under the done-flag race.
candidates.evidence: ws-deque documents Steal::Retry as 'Lost a race with another thief or an emptying pop; the caller should retry' (lib.rs:123) — work is still present. Here `Steal::Empty | Steal::Retry => {}` (line 323) does nothing for both, so after a single contended steal sweep the worker falls through to the done check (line 329) and can break out, dropping still-queued work. Correct work-stealing must distinguish Retry (re-poll) from Empty and only terminate when ALL stealers report Empty AND a real quiescence signal fires.

candidates.summary: Same done-flag termination race in parallel gc_sections BFS: workers can exit before stealing all frontier nodes, under-marking the live set so reachable sections are garbage-collected and dropped from the link.
candidates.evidence: done.store(true, Ordering::Release) (line 1242) fires immediately after spawning the BFS workers that were seeded round-robin (workers[i % pl].push at line 1181). A worker emptying its deque and seeing only Steal::Empty/Retry (line 1210 treats both as no-op) reads done==true (line 1216) and breaks, leaving frontier items in sibling deques unexpanded. Their relocation targets never get added to local_out/results (line 1232), so live.insert (line 1248) never marks them; with --gc-sections those sections are excluded from layout (lines 355-358), yielding a binary missing live code/data. peony-layout has NO tests, so this is uncaught.

candidates.summary: Documented epoch-key fast path is never implemented: try_reuse reads the manifest but never compares manifest.epoch_key, so the advertised 'skip reading any input file' optimization does not exist.
candidates.evidence: The doc comment (lines 283-286) states 'Fast path: compare the epoch key. If it matches, skip reading any input file.' but the body of try_reuse (lines 287-324) only checks version, input path/fingerprint equality (which re-reads every input via Fingerprint::of_file at line 313), and the output fingerprint. epoch_key is stored at line 355 but is read nowhere outside tests. The implementation always takes the slow path, contradicting the documented contract and the module-level epoch-gated architecture claim.

candidates.summary: Stored epoch key is computed with args_hash hardcoded to 0, so the persisted epoch key ignores all link arguments — if the epoch fast-path is ever wired up it will reuse stale output across differing link args.
candidates.evidence: record_link_with_sections computes `let epoch_key = compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0);` (line 352), discarding the args_hash even though compute_epoch_key (line 208) and the tests (epoch_key_differs_by_args_hash, line 544) explicitly require the key to depend on args_hash. CACHE_VERSION is 2 but no caller threads a real args hash through; combined with the unimplemented fast path this makes the persisted epoch_key both unused and semantically wrong. Also note the module doc (line 8) claims SHA-256 while the code uses FNV-1a.

candidates.summary: Relocation writers index the section buffer with no upper-bound check, so a relocation offset near/over the end of a section panics (out-of-bounds slice), aborting the link on malformed/truncated input objects.
candidates.evidence: write_u64 does `buf[off..off + 8].copy_from_slice(...)` (lines 399-400) with no check that off+8 <= buf.len(); write_i32/write_u32/write_u16/write_u8 (lines 403-450) are equally unguarded. apply_reloc sets offset = reloc.offset as usize (line 292) straight from the input InputReloc with no validation against the section length, and patch_buf (line 298) dispatches directly to these writers. A reloc with offset within data_len but offset+width past the end (e.g. an R64 at data_len-4) indexes out of bounds and panics; in emit's parallel path (peony-emit line 356) that panic unwinds inside std::thread::scope and aborts the process. peony-reloc has NO tests covering this.

candidates.summary: section_references_moved is a hardcoded placeholder that colors only .text/.data/.rodata Red on any moved symbol and ignores all other sections, so incremental red-green coloring can return false-Green for sections that actually reference moved symbols, yielding stale output.
candidates.evidence: The function (lines 435-442) ignores section_name relationships and returns `!moved.is_empty() && (section_name == ".text" || ".data" || ".rodata")`. The comment itself admits 'Conservative ... Real implementation: consult the relocation reverse index.' Any output section other than those three (e.g. .text.hot, .data.rel.ro, .init_array, or merged custom sections) whose relocations target a moved symbol will be classified Green by compute_red_green (line 422), so it is skipped on re-emit and retains stale addresses pointing at the symbol's old VA — a silent miscompile in incremental mode. The RelocReverseIndex that exists in this same file (lines 131-200) is never consulted by the coloring path.

candidates.summary: try_reuse keys input identity on the platform-dependent display() string of each path, so the same inputs passed via a different but equivalent path spelling defeat reuse and force a full relink.
candidates.evidence: Stored input keys are `p.display().to_string()` (record at line 349) and compared with `rec_path != &cur.display().to_string()` (line 310). PathBuf::display is lossy and non-canonical: ./a.o vs a.o vs /abs/a.o all compare differently though they name the same file. This makes cache hits brittle across invocation styles and across machines if the manifest is shared, and silently degrades to full links rather than erroring.

findings.summary: Work-stealing termination flag `done` is set true immediately after spawning workers, so idle workers abandon stealing the moment their own deque drains — defeating the advertised load-balanced dispatch (though no work is dropped).
findings.evidence: In write_section_data_parallel, all work items are seeded round-robin into per-worker deques (line 297) BEFORE any thread starts. Inside std::thread::scope, the spawn loop calls the non-blocking scope.spawn (line 311), then `done.store(true, Ordering::Release)` runs at line 365 — i.e. essentially immediately, before workers have processed their items; the real join happens only when the scope closure returns. Each worker loop pops its own deque (line 316), and only consults `done` (line 329) after worker.pop() yields None AND every steal attempt failed. Crucially, lines 322-323 treat `Steal::Empty | Steal::Retry => {}` identically and do NOT retry. Per the ws-deque crate (src/lib.rs steal_from, lines 642-644), `Steal::Retry` is returned on a lost CAS race when items may still exist (the crate's own pop_fifo does `Steal::Retry => continue`). So a worker that drains its own deque, then sees a transient Empty/Retry from each sibling while `done` is already true, breaks out (line 329) instead of waiting for a busy sibling to expose stealable work. No items are dropped — each owner fully drains its own deque via worker.pop(), whose internal pop_fifo retries on contention — but the Chase-Lev work-stealing never rebalances a slow/large section onto an idle thread, so the 'load-balanced dispatch' promised in comments (lines 126, 254-255) does not occur.
findings.fix: Do not flip `done` until producers are truly finished relative to consumers. Since all items are seeded before the scope, either (a) move the `done.store(true)` so a dedicated producer marks completion only after seeding (here seeding already happened, so the flag is effectively useless for balancing), or better (b) restructure the steal loop to keep retrying while any sibling may still hold work: treat `Steal::Retry` as 'retry this victim' (loop/continue without consulting `done`), and only consult `done` after a full pass over all stealers returns `Steal::Empty` for every victim. A robust pattern is to track the count of still-active workers (decrement when a worker is about to exit) and only terminate when the global outstanding-item count reaches zero, rather than a single early-set `done` flag combined with non-retrying steals.
findings.reason: Verified against the source: line numbers, control flow, and the ws-deque steal semantics all match the candidate's description. `done.store(true)` at line 365 runs immediately after the non-blocking spawn loop; the termination check at line 329 is only gated behind a drained own-deque plus failed steals, and lines 322-323 do not retry on Steal::Retry, which the crate documents as a lost-race outcome where items may still exist. The candidate honestly and correctly notes no items are dropped (each owner drains its own deque via the internally-retrying pop), so this is a genuine performance/effectiveness defect rather than a correctness bug — work-stealing is rendered largely inert and the advertised load balancing does not happen. Concrete, reproducible from the code, and worth reporting; medium severity and ~0.78 confidence are appropriate.

findings.summary: In gc_sections' S3-GC parallel BFS, `done.store(true)` runs immediately after spawning workers (before the implicit scope join), so work-stealing is effectively disabled: a worker that empties its own deque and gets one Empty/Retry steal pass exits instead of helping peers, defeating the documented load-balancing guarantee.
findings.evidence: Confirmed against source: `done.store(true, Ordering::Release)` (line 1242) is the final statement of the `std::thread::scope` closure, on the spawning thread, immediately after the for-loop (lines 1191-1240) that spawns all `pl` workers. `std::thread::scope` runs the closure on the calling thread and only joins spawned threads when the closure RETURNS — so `done` becomes true essentially instantly, with no gating on actual work completion. A worker whose own deque empties (`worker.pop()` -> None, lines 1203-1205) makes a single steal pass over `my_stealers` (lines 1207-1212); if all return Empty/Retry it reaches the `done.load` check (line 1216), sees true, and breaks. Thus any worker that drains its round-robin-seeded items faster than a peer exits rather than stealing the peer's backlog, contradicting the doc comment 'thieves steal across them during imbalanced levels' (lines 1100-1101) and the O((m+n)/P + D·logP) bound (line 1103); it degrades to static round-robin partitioning. Correctness is preserved: `worker.pop()` is checked first each iteration and (per ws-deque pop_lifo) only returns None when the deque is truly empty — the last-element CAS race hands the item to a thief who processes it — and children are pushed to thread-local `local_out` (line 1232), never re-seeded onto deques, so no seeded item is dropped and the live set is correct. The identical premature-`done` pattern also exists in peony-emit/src/lib.rs (lines 300/329/365), corroborating the candidate's 'same pattern' framing.
findings.fix: Gate `done` on actual completion rather than setting it right after spawning. E.g., track an `AtomicUsize` count of in-flight/queued items, or have workers spin until all deques (own + all stealers) are observed empty across a full pass before honoring `done`; only set `done=true` once the total outstanding work is known to be zero. Alternatively, since children are accumulated in `local_out` and never re-seeded, this level's deques are a fixed work set — workers should keep attempting steals until every stealer reports Empty in a complete pass (a quiescence check) before exiting, instead of exiting on the first failed pass once the prematurely-set `done` flag is observed.
findings.reason: All candidate claims verified against the actual source and the pinned ws-deque crate. `done.store(true)` at line 1242 truly runs before the scope join, making `done` true almost immediately; a worker that empties its own deque and gets a single failed steal pass exits at line 1216, so cross-worker stealing is effectively inert and the documented load-balancing/complexity guarantee (lines 1100-1103) is not met. The candidate is also correct that correctness is preserved (each worker drains its own deque via pop() before observing done; children go to thread-local local_out and are not re-seeded; ws-deque's pop_lifo only returns None when empty, with last-element races handled by the thief), so no live-set items are dropped. The issue is concrete, reproducible from the code, and worth reporting; medium severity is appropriate since a deliberately-implemented core feature (work-stealing) is silently non-functional and a stated performance bound is violated, even though output is correct.

findings.summary: Input sections named `.got`/`.interp`/`.hash`/etc. are silently dropped: `place()` assigns them `SecSource::Got`/`Interp`/etc. based purely on output-section name, so emit never copies their input bytes.
findings.evidence: Two independent code paths produce builders with the same reserved names. (1) The input-grouping loop (lines 341–398) buckets any allocatable input section named `.got` into `rw_pb` with real `contributions`. (2) The synthetic-section injection (lines 420–433 for `.got`, lines 484–506 for `.interp`/`.hash`/`.dynsym`/`.dynstr`/`.rela.dyn`) pushes *separate* builders with `contributions: Vec::new()` into the same vectors. `place()` then converts every builder whose `.name` matches a reserved string to the corresponding `SecSource` variant (lines 1298–1312), regardless of whether it came from inputs. In emit (peony-emit/src/lib.rs line 220–249), `SecSource::Got` writes GOT slot values, `SecSource::Interp` writes `layout.dyn_blobs.interp`, etc. — none of them iterate `sec.contributions`, so the input bytes from the first builder are silently discarded. `is_allocatable()` (lines 1499–1511) does NOT filter out `.got`, `.interp`, `.hash`, `.dynsym`, `.dynstr`, `.plt`, `.dynamic`, `.rela.*`, `.got.plt` — it only filters `.eh_frame*` and `.note*`.
findings.fix: Assign `SecSource` based on whether the builder originated from the synthetic injection path rather than by name-matching in `place()`. The simplest fix is to add a `source: SecSource` field to `Builder` (defaulting to `SecSource::Input`/`SecSource::Bss`) and set it explicitly when pushing synthetic builders (lines 422, 468, 495, etc.). `place()` then just copies `b.source` instead of pattern-matching on the name. This ensures input contributions are never misclassified as synthetic.
findings.reason: The bug is concrete and traceable through the code: `is_allocatable()` does not block the reserved names, the input-grouping loop creates builders for them with real contributions, `place()` re-classifies them by name to synthetic `SecSource` variants, and emit's match on those variants never touches `contributions`. The scenario requires an input object with a section literally named `.got` (with `SHF_ALLOC`), which is unusual but valid — for example, any object built with a hand-written linker script or assembly. The candidate slightly overstates confidence (claims `.plt` input sections are affected the same way, which is true for dynamic links) and misidentifies where contributions are dropped (they're present in the `OutputSection` but emit ignores them, not that they are removed). The `.note.gnu.build-id` example in the evidence is actually filtered by `is_allocatable()` and thus not affected, but the core `.got`/`.interp`/`.hash` claim is valid.

findings.summary: section_references_moved() hard-codes three section names, allowing non-.text/.data/.rodata sections with moved relocation targets to be incorrectly marked Green
findings.evidence: The function at lines 435-442 returns true only when section_name is exactly ".text", ".data", or ".rodata" and moved is non-empty. For all other sections (e.g., ".got", ".init_array", ".data.rel.ro", ".tdata"), it returns false even if those sections contain relocations against moved symbols. In compute_red_green (line 422), a section is colored Green when its fingerprint matches AND section_references_moved returns false. If a section like .got has unchanged pre-relocation bytes but contains a relocation whose target symbol moved, the relocated bytes in .got will differ, but the section would be incorrectly colored Green ("skip re-emit", line 15). This violates the red-green contract stated at lines 369-371: sections with moved relocation targets must be Red.
findings.fix: Replace the hard-coded section name checks with a proper query against the relocation reverse index (as mentioned in the comment at line 434). The function should return true if ANY relocation in the section references a symbol in the moved set. This requires passing the RelocReverseIndex to section_references_moved and iterating over relocations for each moved symbol to check if any fall within the current section's address range.
findings.reason: The bug is concrete and directly readable from the code. The function implementation contradicts both its own comment (line 432-433 says it checks "could plausibly be referenced") and the red-green coloring contract (lines 369-371). The fix is straightforward but non-trivial. However, confidence is not higher because: (1) no test currently fails (the tests only exercise .text/.rodata), (2) the comment explicitly warns this is temporary, (3) it's unclear whether non-.text sections with relocations are actually generated in practice by the linker, and (3) a production exploit would require a real ELF input that generates a non-.text relocatable section and a moved symbol affecting it. Still, the logical flaw is undeniable.

findings.summary: `try_reuse` never reads `manifest.epoch_key`, so the documented O(1) epoch fast path is absent; every reuse check re-hashes all input files. Compounding this, `record_link_with_sections` writes `epoch_key` with a hard-coded `args_hash = 0`, so the stored epoch key never reflects actual build arguments — making the field doubly dead.
findings.evidence: Lines 283–286 document: "Fast path: compare the epoch key. If it matches, skip reading any input file." The body of `try_reuse` (lines 287–324) decodes the manifest, checks `manifest.version` (line 301) and `manifest.inputs.len()` (line 306), then immediately iterates calling `Fingerprint::of_file(cur)` (line 313) for every input. `manifest.epoch_key` is never accessed. Separately, `record_link_with_sections` (line 352) persists `epoch_key = compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0)` with a hard-coded `args_hash = 0` rather than passing a real args hash, so even if a fast-path check were added, it would still be blind to argument changes.
findings.fix: 1. In `try_reuse`, immediately after version and input-count checks, compute `compute_epoch_key(inputs, args_hash)` and compare it against `manifest.epoch_key`; if they match, skip the per-file fingerprint loop and jump straight to the output fingerprint check (or return `Ok(true)` if the output fingerprint is also trusted). 2. Add an `args_hash: u64` parameter to `record_link_with_sections` (and propagate through `record_link`) so the stored epoch key actually covers build arguments. 3. Add a corresponding `args_hash` parameter to `try_reuse` so the fast-path comparison is apples-to-apples.
findings.reason: Both issues are directly confirmed by the source: `manifest.epoch_key` is declared in `Manifest` (line 261), written at line 355, and never read anywhere in `try_reuse` (lines 287–324). The hard-coded `args_hash = 0` at line 352 is equally unambiguous. The doc claim at lines 283–286 is a clear doc/behavior mismatch, and the performance implication (all inputs re-hashed on every incremental check) is real, though correctness is not broken.

findings.summary: On relocation error, emit_full returns Err after having already written ELF headers and partial section data into the mmap, leaving a corrupt output file on disk with no cleanup.
findings.evidence: Confirmed in source: `write_headers` (line 122) and `write_section_data_parallel` (line 127) both mutate the mmap before the `?` propagates a `RelocError`. The `mmap.flush()` at line 132 is bypassed on the error path. Inspection of memmap2 0.9.10 (`unix.rs:489-498`) confirms `Drop` calls only `munmap`, with no `msync`/flush — but `munmap` on Linux still marks pages for writeback by the page cache, so dirty bytes reach the file. The caller in `peony/src/main.rs:248-249` performs no `std::fs::remove_file` on error. For the `can_overwrite=false` path the file was freshly `create(true).truncate(true)` and sized with `set_len` (lines 102-109); for the in-place path (lines 93-99) the prior binary is partially overwritten. Either way, after a reloc error the file at `output_path` contains a non-functional ELF: a valid header pointing at sections whose relocations were only partially applied.
findings.fix: After `write_section_data_parallel` returns an error, delete the output file before returning: add `let _ = std::fs::remove_file(output_path);` before the `Err` propagation (or wrap the mmap/write steps in a guard that removes the file on drop when in error state). Alternatively, write to a `.tmp` path and rename atomically on success — the canonical approach for linkers (used by GNU ld, lld) that prevents any corrupt file from ever being visible at the final path.
findings.reason: All three legs of the candidate's claim are verified directly from the code and the memmap2 source: (1) mmap is mutated before the error path, (2) flush is only on success, (3) munmap does not msync. The caller does no cleanup. The issue is concrete and reproducible whenever a relocation fails mid-link. Severity is raised from low to medium because a corrupt ELF at the output path could be accidentally executed by a build system that doesn't check the exit code, and because this is the primary deliverable of a linker.

findings.summary: peony-reloc has zero unit tests; patch_buf relocation arithmetic (20+ distinct formulas) is only validated end-to-end via integration tests skipped when system toolchain is absent
findings.evidence: peony-reloc/src/lib.rs contains 0 #[test] functions (confirmed via grep). The patch_buf function (line 298-395) implements 19 distinct relocation type handlers covering: R64, PC64, GOTOFF64, SIZE64, R32/R32S, PC32, SIZE32, PLT32, GOTPCREL/GOTPCRELX/REX_GOTPCRELX, GOT32, GOTPC32, TPOFF32/TPOFF64, DTPOFF32/DTPOFF64, R16/PC16, R8/PC8. Each computes hand-written arithmetic using wrapping_add/sub operations with computed addresses (symbol VA, GOT base, TLS offset, etc.). Test coverage is limited to end-to-end integration tests in peony/tests/relocations.rs and peony/tests/tls.rs, which: (1) assemble x86-64 via the system `as` toolchain, (2) link with peony, (3) execute the binary and inspect exit codes. This means: tests are skipped if the system lacks `as`/cc/ld; patch_buf is never called directly with known inputs/outputs for verification; arithmetic errors (e.g., incorrect sign in GOTOFF64 = S+A-got_base, or negation order in TPOFF32 = tls+A-tls_size) would only be caught if they change the final exit code, not through isolated value checks.
findings.fix: Add a unit test module (#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }) to peony-reloc/src/lib.rs that table-drives over RelocAddrs structures with known inputs and asserts exact output bytes via write_* functions. Example: test GOTOFF64 with S=0x1000, A=-8, got_base=0x2000 expecting little-endian bytes representing 0x1000 + (-8) - 0x2000 = -0x1008. Test TPOFF32 with tls=0x10, A=0, tls_size=0x100 expecting -0xf0. Test each relocation type with at least 2-3 edge cases (zero values, negative results, wrapping arithmetic).
findings.reason: This is a concrete, reproducible defect. The repository structure (grep -c '#[test]' peony-reloc/src/lib.rs returns 0) confirms the claim. The patch_buf function is a core linker component doing complex arithmetic with multiple relocation types; lack of isolated unit tests is a legitimate quality gap. Integration tests provide coarse-grained coverage but cannot catch subtle arithmetic bugs without explicit value assertions. The candidate correctly identifies that test coverage is end-to-end only (tests reference peony/tests/relocations.rs and peony/tests/tls.rs), and correctly notes these are skipped without a system toolchain. The suggested fix (table-driven unit tests with known RelocAddrs and asserted output bytes) is practical and standard practice for arithmetic-heavy code.

findings.summary: write_u32 and related overflow guards lack test coverage; RelocError::Overflow is never asserted in any test
findings.evidence: Line 429 contains: `if (u as u32) as u64 != u && (val as i32) as i64 != val { return Err(overflow(...)) }`. This guard accepts values that fit as either zero-extended u32 OR sign-extended i32. grep confirms RelocError::Overflow is never constructed in tests (only defined at line 453 in peony-reloc/src/lib.rs). The test `mold_arch_x86_64_reloc_overflow` in peony/tests/mold_corpus.rs exists but is marked `#[ignore = "needs unsupported feature"]` and will not execute. No other test suite asserts the Overflow error path. Similar guard functions write_i32, write_u16, write_u8 (lines 403-449) have identical test coverage issues.
findings.fix: Add unit tests in peony-reloc/src/lib.rs that directly test boundary conditions: (1) values that overflow both checks (e.g., 0x1_0000_0000 should fail), (2) boundary values that pass (e.g., 0xFFFF_FFFF as u32, -1 as i32), and (3) values just outside safe ranges. Ensure assertions verify both Ok() and Err(RelocError::Overflow) paths. Example: `assert!(apply_write_u32(buf, 0, 0x1_0000_0000, ...).is_err())` and `assert!(apply_write_u32(buf, 0, 0xFFFF_FFFF, ...).is_ok())`.
findings.reason: Code inspection confirms the overflow guard logic is present and correct in implementation, but test coverage is demonstrably absent. grep of the entire codebase shows RelocError::Overflow is never pattern-matched or asserted in any test. The ignored test placeholder provides no coverage. The candidate's severity assessment of 'high' is justified because a regression (e.g., accidentally dropping the i32 check) would silently change which values truncate vs. overflow with no test failure. The confidence is high (0.92 not 1.0) only because the functional correctness of the current implementation is sound; the defect is purely about test coverage and regression prevention.

findings.summary: Symbol kind (STT_FUNC / STT_OBJECT / STT_TLS) is never captured during parsing; every symbol in the output `.symtab` is emitted as `STT_NOTYPE`, breaking GDB function symbol detection, `nm` type fields, and profiler stack-frame identification.
findings.evidence: In `parse_object` (lines 244-266), `InputSymbol` is constructed without calling `sym.kind()` (available from the `object 0.36` crate). The `InputSymbol` struct (lines 132-146) has no `sym_type` field, so the ELF `STT_FUNC`/`STT_OBJECT`/`STT_TLS` type bits are silently dropped at ingest time. Downstream, `build_symtab_plan` (peony-layout/src/lib.rs lines 1356, 1391, 312) hard-codes `elf::st_info(elf::STB_LOCAL, elf::STT_NOTYPE)` and `elf::st_info(bind, elf::STT_NOTYPE)` for every symbol — local and global alike — because no type information is available. `write_symtab` (peony-emit/src/lib.rs line 483) writes this `info` byte directly to the output symbol table. The `object` crate's `Symbol::kind()` method (which maps `ELF STT` values to `SymbolKind::Text → STT_FUNC`, `SymbolKind::Data → STT_OBJECT`, `SymbolKind::Tls → STT_TLS`, etc.) exists and is accessible but is never called. Concrete impact: (1) `readelf -s` and `nm` show every symbol as type `NOTYPE`; (2) GDB cannot automatically distinguish functions from data labels, breaking `info functions` and some breakpoint workflows; (3) profilers that use `STT_FUNC` to locate frame boundaries (e.g., Linux `perf`, Valgrind callgrind) will fail to identify function extents; (4) for dynamic links, copy relocations for `STT_OBJECT` imports require the size field to be correct — while size IS captured, the type mismatch can cause `ld.so` to mishandle copy relocs.
findings.fix: 1. Add a `sym_type: SymbolKind` field to `InputSymbol` struct (lines 132-146). 2. Call `sym.kind()` in the parsing loop (line 254 context) and capture its value: `sym_type: sym.kind()`. 3. Define a helper function to map `SymbolKind` to ELF symbol type bytes (e.g., `SymbolKind::Text → elf::STT_FUNC`, `SymbolKind::Data → elf::STT_OBJECT`, `SymbolKind::Tls → elf::STT_TLS`). 4. Replace hard-coded `elf::STT_NOTYPE` calls in `build_symtab_plan` (peony-layout lines 1356, 1391, 312) with the mapped type from the `InputSymbol`. 5. Ensure the mapped type byte is included in the `st_info` calculation: `elf::st_info(bind, mapped_type)`. 6. Add a test to verify that a compiled ELF binary contains correct symbol types (e.g., using `readelf -s` or `nm` verification).
findings.reason: The evidence is concrete and verifiable: (1) Compiler verification shows `ObjectSymbol::kind()` method exists and compiles; (2) The `InputSymbol` struct definition is directly readable and demonstrably lacks a type field; (3) Hard-coded `STT_NOTYPE` constants appear at exactly the cited line numbers (1356, 1391, 312); (4) The parse call at line 254 is directly observable and never invokes `sym.kind()`; (5) The write path (peony-emit line 483) directly serializes the `info` field from layout. The three-crate chain (object → layout → emit) is unbroken and leaves no opportunity for type information to enter the output. This is a data-model omission, not a logic error in an otherwise complete path."

findings.summary: build_id_hash hashes all input section bytes (including non-allocatable .debug_*, .symtab, relocation tables, etc.) over pre-relocation data, so the build-id does not identify the output binary; the same linked binary gets different build-ids when compiled with vs without debug info.
findings.evidence: The build_id_hash function (lines 445-462) iterates unconditionally over all sections in objects → obj.sections without filtering. The object parsing (peony-object/src/lib.rs:216-238) includes ALL ELF sections (including .debug_*, relocation sections, .symtab, .strtab marked as SectionKind::Debug and SectionKind::Other). No SHF_ALLOC filtering is applied. The hash is computed in Phase 1 (line 239 of write_section_data_parallel), before Phase 2 applies relocations (lines 252-374). The hash uses raw pre-relocation s.data bytes. Concrete failures: (1) Compiling with -g vs -O2 produces identical loadable segments but different .debug_* bytes → different build-ids for the same runnable binary, defeating build-id's purpose for coredump-debuginfo matching. (2) Relocation sections (.rela.text, non-SHF_ALLOC) are included in the hash. (3) The hash is computed before apply_reloc (line 356) patches section data.
findings.fix: Implement GNU ld's approach: either (a) hash only allocatable output segments after all patching (add SHF_ALLOC flag check, compute hash in Phase 2 after apply_reloc completes), or (b) write a 16-byte placeholder in Phase 1 and compute the final hash over the complete output ELF in a Phase 3 before flush. Ensure relocation sections and debug sections are excluded from the hash. The hash should represent the loadable output binary, not the input objects' metadata.
findings.reason: Direct code inspection confirms: (1) build_id_hash iterates obj.sections unconditionally (line 451 has no filtering), (2) obj.sections includes all parsed ELF sections including .debug_* (classify_section handles these), (3) hash is computed in Phase 1 (line 239), before Phase 2 relocations (lines 252-374), (4) no SHF_ALLOC check exists, (5) the candidate's phase ordering and section inclusion claims are verifiable from the code structure. The only uncertainty is whether relocation sections are actually materialized in obj.sections (they appear to be parsed as SectionKind::Other), but the fundamental issue—hashing all sections before relocations—is definitively present and problematic.

findings.summary: `atomic_write` uses a fixed, process-independent temp path (`manifest.tmp`), creating a write-write race when two concurrent `peony` processes target the same output file with `--incremental` enabled.
findings.evidence: At line 447, `let tmp = path.with_extension("tmp")` converts `<output>.incr/manifest.bin` → `<output>.incr/manifest.tmp`. This is a deterministic, process-independent path. If two `peony` invocations with `--incremental` run concurrently against the same `-o <output>` (e.g. a misconfigured parallel build rule), both call `std::fs::write(&tmp, data)` on the identical path. `fs::write` is not atomic (it truncates then writes in multiple syscalls), so interleaved writes produce a corrupt file. The subsequent `std::fs::rename(&tmp, path)` at line 452 is POSIX-atomic, so exactly one rename wins, but may commit a file containing bytes from both processes. No file locking or PID-based uniqueness guards this path; the crate has no `flock`/`Mutex`/`LockFile` dependency. Confirmed by: `manifest_path` (line 277-279) = `cache_dir(output).join("manifest.bin")`; `atomic_write` called only from `record_link_with_sections` (line 364); `record_link` called once per process at main.rs:252 after the full link completes.
findings.fix: Replace the fixed extension with a per-process unique name before the atomic rename: e.g. `let tmp = path.with_file_name(format!("manifest.{}.tmp", std::process::id()))`, or use `tempfile::NamedTempFile::new_in(path.parent().unwrap())` (same directory required for `rename` atomicity). Either approach ensures no two concurrent writers share the same temp path, making the write-then-rename pattern genuinely atomic.
findings.reason: The code defect is real and confirmed: line 447 produces a deterministic temp path with no per-process uniqueness. The race condition analysis is correct — `fs::write` is not atomic and two concurrent writers on the same path can corrupt the file before the winning rename. Confidence is slightly reduced from the candidate's 0.88 because the triggering scenario (two `peony` processes targeting the *same output file simultaneously* with `--incremental`) is narrow and requires a build system misconfiguration; standard `make -j` would not normally schedule two rules writing the same output. The severity of "low" is confirmed — this is not a security issue, affects only the incremental cache (a correctness hint, not a runtime invariant), and the common single-process case is entirely unaffected. The fix described in the candidate is accurate.

findings.summary: `write_u16` and `write_u8` hard-code the wrong `r_type` constant in their overflow error: `PC16` overflows are reported as `R16`, and `PC8` overflows are reported as `R8`, making relocation overflow errors misleading when diagnosing PC-relative reference failures.
findings.evidence: `write_u16` (lines 436-442) constructs an overflow error via `overflow(object, reloc_off, val, r_x86_64::R16)`. It is called from two sites: the `R16` arm (line 385) and the `PC16` arm (line 386). The `PC16` call passes the same `r_x86_64::R16` constant (= 12) rather than `r_x86_64::PC16` (= 13), so a PC-relative 16-bit overflow reports the wrong type. Similarly, `write_u8` (lines 444-450) always uses `r_x86_64::R8` (= 14), but is also called from the `PC8` arm (line 388) where the correct constant would be `r_x86_64::PC8` (= 15). The `RelocError::Overflow` message on line 36 includes the `r_type` field (`'… for type {r_type}'`), so a user or tool seeing the error would look up the wrong relocation kind, leading them to diagnose the wrong relocation issue.
findings.fix: Modify `write_u16` and `write_u8` to accept `r_type` as a parameter (as `write_i32` does on line 407), and update the call sites (lines 385, 386, 387, 388) to pass the appropriate relocation type constant.
findings.reason: The bug is verifiable by direct inspection of the call sites (lines 385-388) and the helper implementations (lines 436-446). The constants are defined clearly (R16=12, PC16=13, R8=14, PC8=15), and the hard-coding of the wrong values is explicit in the code. The impact is on error reporting—misleading error messages in a linker are directly harmful to developers diagnosing relocation failures.

dismissed.summary: `output_section_name` collapses `.data.rel.ro` into `.data`, but this is the function's documented, intended behavior — not a defect; the lack of RELRO hardening is a separate unimplemented feature, not a bug at this line.
dismissed.evidence: Empirically confirmed: output_section_name(".data.rel.ro") returns ".data" (lines 1525-1532 strip the suffix after the second '.', as the doc comment at 1523-1524 and research/SPEC.md:226 both state is intended). However: (1) The function's contract is explicitly "strip suffix after second '.'", so `.data.rel.ro → .data` is by design, identical in spirit to `.text.unlikely → .text`. (2) The merge is NOT the cause of any hardening loss. `.data.rel.ro` carries SHF_WRITE, so perm_of() (lines 1513-1521) classifies it as Perm::Rw regardless; it is bucketed into the RW PT_LOAD at lines 408-412/943-954. (3) The layout emits only RO/RX/RW PT_LOAD segments plus PT_NOTE/INTERP/DYNAMIC — there is NO PT_GNU_RELRO anywhere in build (verified lines 909-1000). RELRO is entirely unimplemented: all peony/tests/mold_corpus.rs relro tests (mold_relro, mold_relro_alignment, mold_copyrel_relro) are #[ignore]. So even if the merge were removed, `.data.rel.ro` would still land in a permanently-writable segment with no RELRO protection. The outcome is identical with or without the merge.
dismissed.fix: No fix at this line is warranted. The function correctly implements its documented grouping contract. RELRO support (a distinct relro output-section bucket plus a PT_GNU_RELRO segment) is a missing feature tracked by the ignored mold_relro* corpus tests, not a regression introduced by output_section_name. Report it as a feature gap if RELRO is in scope, but it is not a bug in this code.
dismissed.reason: The candidate's factual mechanics are correct (`.data.rel.ro` does collapse to `.data`, verified by compiling the function), but it mischaracterizes intended behavior as a regression. The function honors its documented contract ("strip suffix after second '.'", per its doc comment and SPEC.md:226). The 'permanently writable relro' outcome is caused by RELRO being entirely unimplemented — the layout builds no PT_GNU_RELRO segment and has only RO/RX/RW permission classes, and all relro corpus tests are #[ignore]. Because `.data.rel.ro` is SHF_WRITE it is bucketed as Perm::Rw and would share the writable RW segment even if kept as a distinct section, so this line is not the cause. There is no concrete, fixable defect here; it is a missing feature, not a bug. The candidate itself rates severity low and confidence 0.55 and concedes the program runs correctly.

dismissed.summary: PIE mode sets ET_DYN but does not force base_address to 0, so a PIE produced with the default LayoutConfig is laid out at the fixed VA 0x400000 contradicting the documented 'base 0' PIE contract.
dismissed.evidence: The candidate claims `compute_layout` uses `base = config.base_address` unconditionally (line 287) and that `LayoutConfig::default()` (`base_address: 0x40_0000`) combined with `pie: true` would produce broken PIE output. However, `peony/src/main.rs` lines 151–157 explicitly override this: `let base_address = if args.pie { 0 } else { parse_hex_or_dec(&args.base_address) … };`. The `0` is injected before `LayoutConfig` is constructed (line 217–222), so the default `base_address` field is never consulted for PIE links in the actual driver. No caller constructs `LayoutConfig { pie: true, base_address: 0x400000, .. }` directly.
dismissed.fix: No fix needed — the driver already enforces base 0 for PIE. Optionally, the library could add a debug assertion (`debug_assert!(!(config.pie && config.base_address != 0))`) or clamp `base` to 0 internally when `config.pie` is true, to protect hypothetical future callers who use `compute_layout` directly with a misconfigured `LayoutConfig`.
dismissed.reason: The candidate's core premise is factually wrong. The driver at `peony/src/main.rs:152-157` explicitly sets `base_address = 0` whenever `--pie` is active, before constructing `LayoutConfig`. The library-level gap (no internal clamping) is a theoretical concern for library misuse, not a concrete, reproducible bug in the current codebase. The issue does not exist as described.

dismissed.summary: Candidate claim about weak-undef PC32 producing spurious overflow is not supported by the code or linker semantics.
dismissed.evidence: Line 253 sets s=0 for an unresolved weak global. The PC32 arm (lines 315-322) then computes 0 + a - p. For a typical non-PIE executable with base 0x400000, section VAs sit well below 2 GB; e.g. p=0x401010 with addend=-4 gives -4 198 420, which fits comfortably in i32 and passes write_i32. Overflow would only occur if p > ~2^31, i.e. sections placed above the 2 GB boundary — not the normal case for this linker's default 0x400000 base. Furthermore, GNU ld uses the identical formula (S+A-P with S=0) for PC-relative references to weak-undef symbols; it does not special-case the result to 0. The candidate's claim that "the conventional ld behavior is to emit 0 without erroring" is factually incorrect: both GNU ld and the ABI spec compute S+A-P with S=0, producing a large negative i32 that happens to fit in normal address ranges.
dismissed.fix: No fix warranted. The computation matches ELF ABI semantics and GNU ld behavior. If support for executables with sections above 2 GB is ever added, a targeted guard could be added, but that is a separate feature scope.
dismissed.reason: The candidate incorrectly characterises the conventional linker behaviour (GNU ld computes 0+A-P, not 0), and the overflow condition it describes (p > 2 GB) does not arise for standard non-PIE executables at the linker's default 0x400000 base. The code at line 253 and the PC32 arm are both correct for the intended use case. The issue is not reproducible under normal operating conditions and the underlying semantic premise is wrong.

dismissed.summary: Candidate overstates coverage gap; existing integration test directly guards the weak-undefined-to-zero branch.
dismissed.evidence: The code at line 253 (`Some(_) if sym.binding == Binding::Weak => (0, 0, 0, 0)`) is real, and there are zero `#[test]` items in `peony-reloc/src/lib.rs`. However, `peony/tests/symbols.rs::weak_undefined_is_zero` (line 29) is a plain `#[test]` (no `#[ignore]`) that assembles an object with `.weak missing`, links it with peony, runs the binary, and asserts exit code 42 — which is only possible if the weak-undefined symbol was resolved to 0 and not errored. A behavior flip on line 253 (returning `UndefinedSymbol` instead of `(0,0,0,0)`) would immediately fail this test at the `link()` call. The candidate correctly identifies the absence of an isolated unit test and the toolchain dependency, but the key claim — that a behavior flip "current tests could miss" — is materially incorrect given this integration test.
dismissed.fix: No fix required; the existing integration test is sufficient to guard this semantic. Adding an in-crate unit test with a mock symbol table and a mock layout context would be a nice-to-have for faster, toolchain-free feedback, but the issue as filed overstates the regression risk.
dismissed.reason: The candidate's central claim — that a behavior flip in the weak-undefined branch "could miss" detection — is falsified by `peony/tests/symbols.rs::weak_undefined_is_zero`, which is a non-ignored `#[test]` that exercises exactly this branch end-to-end and would fail loudly if the branch were changed to error instead of zero. The gap is real (no isolated unit test, external toolchain needed), but it does not rise to a reportable defect; it is at most a low-priority nicety. Marking invalid because the specific risk claim in the evidence is demonstrably wrong.

dismissed.summary: align_up lacks a dedicated unit test and could overflow near u64::MAX
dismissed.evidence: Verified facts: align_up is at line 1535 as `let align = align.max(1); (val + align - 1) & !(align - 1)`, peony-layout/src/lib.rs has 0 #[test] functions, and align_up is used widely (lines 389,448,602,611,634,750,781,797,1268). The root Cargo.toml has no [profile] overflow override. However, several of the candidate's claims are inaccurate or speculative: (1) 'silently wraps on overflow' is only true in release mode — in debug/test builds `val + align - 1` panics on overflow, so it does not silently corrupt anything during testing; (2) overflow near u64::MAX is not reachable from realistic inputs — `val` is either a virtual address starting at base (default 0x40_0000, line 235) growing by cumulative section sizes, or a bounded section/TLS offset; hitting u64::MAX would require exabyte-scale inputs or an absurd base_address config, and the candidate gives no reproducible path; (3) the alignment invariant is already covered end-to-end: peony/tests/features.rs::section_alignment (line 100) links a `.p2align 12` symbol and asserts `val % 0x1000 == 0`, and there are dedicated tls.rs and algorithms.rs integration suites. align_up is a private 2-line helper whose behavior is already exercised by the integration tests, consistent with this project's deliberate end-to-end testing convention.
dismissed.fix: No change warranted. If desired one could add a trivial #[cfg(test)] mod asserting align_up(0x1001,0x1000)==0x2000, idempotence on aligned values, and align==0 -> treated as 1, but this is optional polish rather than a defect fix. Overflow is already a panic in debug/test builds and unreachable from real inputs.
dismissed.reason: The candidate overstates the issue. 'Silently wraps on overflow' is false for the debug/test build mode (it panics), and the overflow-near-u64::MAX scenario is not reproducible from any realistic input or default config — it is purely theoretical. The alignment invariant the candidate claims is untested is in fact covered by peony/tests/features.rs::section_alignment and the broader integration suite, matching the project's end-to-end testing convention. A missing dedicated unit test for a trivial private 2-line helper whose behavior is already exercised is not a concrete, worth-reporting defect, so valid=false.

dismissed.summary: Missing unit tests for output_section_name is a real observation but does not constitute a code defect worth reporting as a review comment
dismissed.evidence: The function output_section_name (line 1525) exists and its logic is confirmed: it skips the first character, finds the next '.', and returns the prefix up to and including it. Verified mappings: '.data.rel.ro'->'.data', '.text.hot'->'.text', '.init_array'->'.init_array', '.bss'->'.bss'. There are zero occurrences of '#[cfg(test)]' or 'mod tests' in peony-layout/src/lib.rs. The integration test suite has stub placeholders for section-name tests (mold_arch_x86_64_section_name, mold_many_output_sections) but they are all marked #[ignore]. The function is private and only called in one place (line 368). The behavior described by the candidate is accurate.
dismissed.fix: Add a #[cfg(test)] mod tests block to peony-layout/src/lib.rs with direct unit tests for output_section_name covering: simple names (.bss, .text, .data), compound names (.text.hot, .data.rel.ro, .rodata.str1.1, .text._ZN...), and edge cases (.init_array, ..L__unnamed double-dot).
dismissed.reason: The candidate identifies a real gap (no unit tests for output_section_name) and accurately describes the function's behavior. However, 'missing-test' is not a code defect in the sense that would be reported as a concrete, actionable review comment against the diff. The issue is that: (1) there is no *new* code introduced in the diff being reviewed that specifically worsened test coverage for this function — it existed prior; (2) the category 'missing-test' is a general quality concern rather than a concrete bug or correctness issue reproducible from the code; (3) the function's logic is straightforward and consistent with what a standard ELF linker does — the name-prefix grouping is intentional and documented in the function's doc comment; (4) the claimed severity of 'sections landing in the wrong segment' does not follow from a missing test but would require an actual logic bug, which the candidate does not identify. The observation is valid as a general codebase health note but does not meet the bar of 'concrete, reproducible from the code, and worth reporting' as a diff-level review comment.

sweep_notes: peony-object/src/lib.rs:254 Symbol kind (STT_FUNC / STT_OBJECT / STT_TLS) is never captured during parsing; every symbol in the output `.symtab` is emitted as `STT_NOTYPE`, breaking GDB function symbol detection, `nm` type fields, and profiler stack-frame identification.

peony-emit/src/lib.rs:451 `build_id_hash` hashes all input section bytes (including non-allocatable `.debug_*`, `.symtab`, relocation tables, etc.) over pre-relocation data, so the build-id does not identify the output binary; the same linked binary gets different build-ids when compiled with vs without debug info.

peony-cache/src/lib.rs:447 `atomic_write` derives the temp path as `manifest.bin → manifest.tmp` (a fixed, process-independent path), creating a write-write race when two concurrent linker invocations target the same output file (e.g., parallel `make -j` with a shared output).

peony-reloc/src/lib.rs:438 `write_u16` and `write_u8` hard-code the wrong `r_type` constant in their overflow error: `PC16` overflows are reported as `R16`, and `PC8` overflows are reported as `R8`, making relocation overflow errors misleading when diagnosing PC-relative reference failures.
result.final_report:
The report is complete. Here's a quick orientation:

**12 findings total** across 5 crates — 4 high, 5 medium, 3 low — with inline code comments attached at the relevant line ranges.

**The four high-severity items deserve immediate attention:**

- **#1 (STT_NOTYPE)** is the most impactful: the entire symbol type information pipeline is broken end-to-end, meaning every linked binary is opaque to GDB, `nm`, and profilers. The fix is mechanical (add one field, call one method, propagate through three sites).

- **#2 (build-id)** produces build-ids that are unstable across debug/non-debug builds of the same binary — directly breaking the coredump → debuginfo toolchain.

- **#3 and #4 (reloc test coverage)** are pure test-debt: `peony-reloc` has *zero* `#[test]` functions protecting 19 hand-coded arithmetic formulas and an overflow guard that no CI run can regress.

**The two concurrency findings (#6, #7)** share the exact same root cause — `done.store(true)` fires on the spawner before the scope join — so a single understanding yields a fix in both files. Correctness is unaffected, but the documented work-stealing / load-balancing behaviour simply doesn't happen.
