candidates.summary: Incremental cache key ignores linker arguments — changing args reuses a stale binary
candidates.evidence: try_reuse() only compares manifest.version, input path+fingerprint pairs, and the output fingerprint (lines 301-323). It never receives or checks any representation of the linker arguments. record_link_with_sections() stores epoch_key = compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0) (line 352) with args_hash hardcoded to 0, and record_link() (used by the driver, main.rs:252) passes no sections/symbols. The driver calls try_reuse(&args.output, &inputs) with `inputs` being only resolved file paths. Therefore `peony foo.o -o a.out --incremental` followed by `peony foo.o -o a.out --gc-sections --entry main --incremental` will hit the cache (inputs + output fingerprint unchanged) and silently reuse the previous binary that does NOT reflect the new arguments. The crate doc advertises the epoch key as SHA-256(mtimes || args_hash) (lines 6-10), but args_hash is never plumbed through, so this invariant is violated.

candidates.summary: Relocation writers never bounds-check off+N against buf.len(), panicking on out-of-range reloc offsets
candidates.evidence: write_u64 (buf[off..off+8], line 400), write_i32 (buf[off..off+4], 415), write_u32 (432), write_u16 (440), and write_u8 (448) index the buffer using off = reloc.offset as usize (set in apply_reloc line 292) but only validate the value's width (overflow), never that off+N <= buf.len(). In peony-emit (lib.rs:345-352) the slice handed to apply_reloc is exactly isec.data.len() bytes. A malformed or crafted object file whose relocation offset is near or past the section end (e.g. an R64 reloc at section_size-4, or any offset >= data_len) makes buf[off..off+8] panic with a slice-index out of bounds. That panic occurs inside std::thread::scope in emit, so it is re-raised on join and aborts the link after the output file has been partially written. By contrast the legacy/serial writers in peony-emit (write_bytes line 494, GOT line 231, write_symtab line 468) DO guard with `if off + N <= buf.len()`. The reloc path lacks any equivalent guard.
candidates.angle: bounds checking on untrusted ELF input

candidates.summary: section_references_moved uses a hardcoded section-name heuristic that mis-colors sections (false Green / false Red)
candidates.evidence: section_references_moved() returns `!moved.is_empty() && (section_name == ".text" || section_name == ".data" || section_name == ".rodata")`. Two logic errors: (1) FALSE GREEN — any output section whose name is not exactly one of those three (e.g. .init_array, .fini_array, .tdata, .got, .data.rel.ro, .tbss) is treated as NOT referencing a moved symbol, so compute_red_green (line 422) colors it Green and would skip re-emitting it even when it actually contains relocations against a moved symbol, producing a stale/incorrect binary. (2) FALSE RED — whenever ANY symbol moved, every .text/.data/.rodata section is marked Red regardless of whether it references the moved symbol, defeating the incremental benefit. The function ignores both the section's real reloc edges and the RelocReverseIndex that exists for this purpose. The comment admits it is a placeholder. compute_red_green is currently only exercised by tests, not wired into the driver, which limits present-day impact, but the coloring logic is incorrect as written.

candidates.summary: Work-stealing loop treats Steal::Retry like Empty, harming load balancing (Retry means data is present)
candidates.evidence: In the steal loop `match s.steal() { Steal::Success(v) => ..., Steal::Empty | Steal::Retry => {} }` (lines 321-324; identical pattern in peony-layout lib.rs:1208-1211). ws-deque defines Steal::Retry as 'the deque is non-empty but you lost a race; the caller should retry' (ws-deque/src/lib.rs:123-124), distinct from Steal::Empty. By collapsing Retry into the no-op arm, a thief abandons a victim that demonstrably has stealable work, then (combined with `done` being set early, line 365) may exit via the done.load() branch (line 329) without redistributing load. This does not lose work — each Worker drains its own queue via pop() before exiting, so all seeded items are still processed by their owner — but it degrades the work-stealing balance the design relies on (QUAD Theorem 5.1). Correct handling is to retry the same stealer on Retry.

candidates.summary: GOTPCREL/GOT reference to an imported (undefined-locally) symbol allocates no GOT slot and emits no GLOB_DAT
candidates.evidence: scan_object gates GOT-slot creation on `needs_got(reloc.r_type) && res.is_defined()` (line 182). For a GOTPCREL/GOTPCRELX/REX_GOTPCRELX reference to a dynamic import (res.is_defined() == false), no SyntheticSlot::Got is recorded, so the import gets neither a GOT slot nor a GLOB_DAT entry (layout n_glob_dat counts only got_syms that are imports, but got_syms never contains imports). At apply time, GOTPCREL uses a.g (got_address), which stays 0 for that symbol, yielding a bogus PC-relative value into a nonexistent GOT slot. For the primary fully-static link path this cannot trigger, but the dynamic-executable path (DynamicInfo present) would mis-link any imported symbol referenced through the GOT rather than the PLT.

candidates.summary: Untrusted on-disk manifest is bincode-decoded without size/allocation limits
candidates.evidence: try_reuse (lines 296-300) and compute_red_green (392-401) decode the persisted manifest with bincode::serde::decode_from_slice(&bytes, bincode::config::standard()). The standard config imposes no limit on collection lengths, so a corrupted or hostile manifest.bin (e.g. a Vec length field of 2^60) can drive a huge pre-allocation / OOM before any validation. Decode errors are handled gracefully (Ok(false)), but an attacker-controlled-but-syntactically-valid length is not bounded. Impact is limited because the cache directory is local and writable only by the user, but the QUAD/SPEC notes call out untrusted-input deserialization as a concern.

candidates.summary: Documented epoch-key fast path is never executed; try_reuse always re-fingerprints every input
candidates.evidence: The module + function docs (lines 5-10, 283-286) promise an O(1) epoch-gated fast path: 'compare the epoch key. If it matches, skip reading any input file.' In practice try_reuse never loads or compares manifest.epoch_key — it always falls through to the slow path, calling Fingerprint::of_file(cur) on every input (line 313) and on the output (320), i.e. reading and hashing every byte. The epoch_key field is written (line 356) but only ever read in tests. This makes the claimed Theorem 6.2 fast path dead code and the per-link cost O(total input bytes) rather than O(mtimes).

candidates.summary: Overwrite-in-place test only relinks identical inputs, so it cannot catch stale-byte corruption from the non-zeroing fast path
candidates.evidence: overwrite_in_place_produces_identical_output (line 162) links the SAME object twice, so bytes1==bytes2 trivially. In peony-emit/src/lib.rs:117-119 the buffer is only zeroed when `!can_overwrite`; the overwrite-in-place branch (can_overwrite==true, lines 93-99) reuses the existing file contents WITHOUT zeroing. A second link that produces the same file_size but differs in padding/gap/BSS-image bytes (e.g. a section shrinks while another grows, or relocations no longer overwrite a previously-patched byte) would leave stale bytes from the prior link. No test ever relinks DIFFERENT content into a same-sized file and diffs against a fresh (truncating) link, so a stale-byte regression in the TLB-aware path passes silently.

candidates.summary: No test exercises relocation overflow detection or the signed/unsigned range-check logic in write_i32/write_u32/write_u16/write_u8
candidates.evidence: peony-reloc has zero #[test] functions and no tests module. The only corpus reference, mold_arch_x86_64_reloc_overflow (peony/tests/mold_corpus.rs:491-495), is #[ignore]d with an empty body. The width-checked writers contain subtle range logic - e.g. write_u32 (line 419-434) accepts a value if EITHER the zero-extended u32 round-trips OR the sign-extended i32 round-trips, and write_i32 (line 411-413) checks `v as i64 != val`. A regression that flips a comparison (e.g. && vs ||, or drops a branch) would let an out-of-range PC32/PLT32/R32S value be silently truncated into the output with no test failing, producing a miscompiled binary. The review focus explicitly calls out verifying PC32/PLT32/GOT range checks and signed/unsigned handling, none of which is covered.

candidates.summary: Red-green coloring tests never pass a non-empty moved_symbols set, leaving the moved-symbol to Red path (the core Theorem 6.1 mechanism) untested
candidates.evidence: Both red_green_all_green_when_nothing_changes (line 246) and red_green_changed_section_is_red (line 288) call compute_red_green(&fake_output, &current, &[]) with an empty moved_symbols slice. The branch in peony-cache/src/lib.rs:422 that forces a fingerprint-identical section to Red when section_references_moved(name,&moved) is true (lines 435-442) is never executed. A regression that disables moved-symbol invalidation (e.g. section_references_moved always returns false) would still pass both tests, yet would incorrectly mark a section Green and skip re-emit even though one of its relocation targets moved - exactly the unsound case Theorem 6.1 guards against.

candidates.summary: Incremental cache test only covers the cache-HIT path; no test verifies that a changed input invalidates the cache and produces a correct different output
candidates.evidence: incremental_cache_full_reuse (line 359) links identical inputs twice and asserts bytes1==bytes2 (cache hit). There is no test for try_reuse returning false: link with --incremental, MODIFY the input object, relink, and assert the output changed and the binary still runs correctly. peony-cache/src/lib.rs:287-324 (try_reuse) decides reuse by re-fingerprinting every input and the output; a regression where it returns true too eagerly (e.g. skips an input fingerprint comparison, or matches stale output) would reuse a wrong binary after a real source change and every existing test would still pass - a silent incremental miscompile.

candidates.summary: Multi-level GC chain test asserts only exit code 0 and never verifies the dead section is dropped, so an over-keeping GC regression is undetectable
candidates.evidence: s3gc_chain_multiple_bfs_levels (line 70) docstring claims 'any extra sections beyond these four must be dead', but the body's only assertion is assert_eq!(run(&out), 0) at line 113. The fixture defines dead_fn in .text.dead which is never referenced. A GC bug that keeps ALL sections (defeating GC entirely) still exits 0 and passes. Unlike s3gc_live_set_is_correct_and_smaller, this test does no size comparison and no readelf/symbol inspection, so it cannot prove the multi-level BFS actually drops dead sections - only that whatever survives runs. The 'live set is exactly correct' claim is unverified.

candidates.summary: Determinism test uses a single object with no GOT/PLT slots and no weak symbols, so it barely exercises the parallel reloc-apply work-stealing nondeterminism it claims to cover
candidates.evidence: parallel_emit_is_deterministic (line 121) comments 'multiple sections, relocations, weak symbols' but the det.s fixture (lines 125-138) declares only global symbols x and _start with no .weak and produces no GOT slots. The actual nondeterminism risk is in write_section_data_parallel (peony-emit/src/lib.rs:296-366) distributing many work items across many objects/threads via the ws-deque, plus the serial GOT fill (lines 224-234) ordered by layout.got_slots. With one object and no GOT slots, thread interleaving is minimal, so a real ordering bug (e.g. relocation apply order affecting overlapping writes, or GOT slot ordering depending on thread schedule) would likely not surface. The determinism claim (Theorem 12.1) needs a fixture with multiple objects, GOTPCREL/PLT32 relocations, and weak symbols to be meaningful.

candidates.summary: RelocReverseIndex concurrent test never reads while writers run and never exercises the silent out-of-bounds drop path
candidates.evidence: reloc_reverse_index_concurrent_correctness (line 320) joins all inserter threads (thread::scope ends at line 340) BEFORE any iter_relocs call, so the Acquire/Release ordering between concurrent insert (peony-cache/src/lib.rs:150-168) and iter_relocs (lines 171-200) is never tested under true concurrency - only after a full barrier. Additionally, insert silently returns without inserting when symbol_id>=heads.len() or reloc_id>=nexts.len() (lines 152-154); no test inserts an out-of-range reloc_id/symbol_id to confirm it is dropped (not panicking and not corrupting another chain). A regression weakening the CAS ordering to Relaxed, or mis-sizing the bounds check, would pass this test.

candidates.summary: Undefined-weak symbol resolution to address 0 is never tested, so a regression turning it into a hard error (or vice versa) would be missed
candidates.evidence: apply_reloc resolves an unresolved Weak symbol to (s,g,l,z)=(0,0,0,0) at line 253, while a non-weak undefined symbol returns RelocError::UndefinedSymbol (lines 254-259). No test in the suite assembles a weak undefined reference and asserts the link succeeds with the reloc resolved against address 0 (e.g. PC32 to a null weak), nor asserts that a non-weak undefined symbol is rejected with UndefinedSymbol. The algorithms.rs determinism fixture mentions 'weak symbols' but contains none. A regression that drops the Binding::Weak special-case (line 253) would turn valid weak-undef links into errors with nothing catching it.

candidates.summary: Work-stealing termination flag is set to true immediately after spawning, defeating load-balancing and contradicting the QUAD Theorem 5.1 parallelism guarantee
candidates.evidence: Inside `std::thread::scope`, `done.store(true, Ordering::Release)` (line 365) runs on the main thread right after the spawn `for` loop — it does NOT wait for workers. So `done` becomes true almost instantly. Each worker only checks `done` after `worker.pop()` returns None AND every steal returned Empty/Retry (lines 326-332). Chase-Lev `steal()` returns Steal::Empty/Retry transiently under contention even when a victim still has items; with `done` already true the thief breaks instead of helping. Correctness is preserved only because each worker drains its OWN deque via pop() before ever checking `done` (ws-deque pop_lifo, src/lib.rs:530); items are seeded round-robin (line 297) so every item has an owner. Net effect: the work-stealing degrades to static round-robin partitioning — the documented zero-sync parallel relocation apply is largely cosmetic, and any future refactor that stops draining the owner deque first would silently drop section writes/relocations.

candidates.summary: Identical premature done-flag store defeats the S3-GC parallel BFS work-stealing (same bug as peony-emit)
candidates.evidence: `done.store(true, Ordering::Release)` (line 1242) executes on the main thread immediately after the spawn loop inside `std::thread::scope`, before threads join. Worker threads only consult `done` (line 1216) after their own deque is empty and all steals returned Empty/Retry. Because `done` is true from the outset, a worker that empties its deque and hits one transient Empty/Retry steal exits instead of stealing the still-pending frontier of a slower sibling. GC correctness is retained (each worker drains its own deque via pop() first and pushes to local_out, which is later deduped into the next frontier at lines 1246-1251), but the adaptive parallel BFS (Pl threads per level) provides little real load balancing, undercutting the QUAD §3 parallel-GC performance claim.

candidates.summary: Documented epoch-key fast path is never implemented: try_reuse ignores manifest.epoch_key and always falls through to the slow per-file fingerprint path
candidates.evidence: The doc comment on `try_reuse` (lines 283-286) and QUAD.md §6 (research/QUAD.md:444-446 `if epoch_key == cache.epoch_key: skip`) promise: 'Fast path: compare the epoch key. If it matches, skip reading any input file.' But the function body never reads `manifest.epoch_key`; it always re-reads and re-fingerprints every input file (lines 309-317) plus the output (lines 320-323). The persisted `epoch_key` field is therefore dead: it is written by record_link_with_sections (line 355) but read by nothing in the codebase (grep confirms only definitions/tests reference it). The advertised O(1) incremental fast path does not exist, so every `--incremental` relink pays full O(|inputs|) I/O+hash cost — a functional/performance deviation from the documented contract.

candidates.summary: Persisted epoch_key is computed with args_hash hardcoded to 0, so it never reflects link arguments — args changes cannot invalidate the epoch
candidates.evidence: `record_link_with_sections` computes `let epoch_key = compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0);` (line 352), always passing args_hash = 0. The epoch design (QUAD Definition 6.2, and the module doc line 8: 'SHA-256(sorted file mtimes ∥ args_hash)') requires the args hash to be part of the key so that re-linking with different flags (e.g. different `-o`, GC on/off) invalidates the epoch. With args_hash fixed at 0 the stored key is identical for any argument set. This is currently masked because try_reuse never reads epoch_key (line 287 finding) and the driver uses record_link (peony/src/main.rs:252) which records no sections/symbols, but it makes the persisted epoch semantically wrong and will mis-validate once the fast path is wired up.

candidates.summary: section_references_moved ignores its symbol argument and hard-codes section names, so red-green coloring mis-classifies any non-.text/.data/.rodata section and is unsound for renamed/custom output sections
candidates.evidence: `section_references_moved` (lines 435-442) never consults the relocation reverse index it documents; it returns `section_name == ".text" || ".data" || ".rodata"` whenever any symbol moved. Consequence: (a) a section like `.text.hot`, `.init`, or any custom-named output section that DOES reference a moved symbol is reported as not-referencing-moved → would be colored Green and skipped on re-emit, which is unsound if this coloring ever gates incremental re-emit; (b) over-coloring the three hard-coded names Red even when they do not reference the moved symbol wastes work. The function is currently only exercised by unit tests (compute_red_green is never called from the driver), but as written it cannot correctly drive incremental re-emit.

candidates.summary: RelocReverseIndex::insert silently drops out-of-range (symbol_id, reloc_id) pairs instead of signaling, risking a silently-incomplete reverse index
candidates.evidence: `insert` (lines 150-154) returns early with no error/log when `sym_idx >= heads.len()` or `reloc_id >= nexts.len()`. The index is sized once via `new(symbol_count, reloc_count)` (line 141); any caller that under-sizes the arrays (e.g. counts computed before late symbol/reloc additions, or an off-by-one) gets a reverse index that is missing entries with zero diagnostics. Since iter_relocs is intended to drive which sections are re-emitted (QUAD §10.3), a silently-dropped insert would under-color Red sections and skip required re-emits. The lock-free CAS loop itself is correct (Relaxed load/store of next + Release CAS on head, Acquire on read), but the bounds-drop is a latent correctness hazard worth surfacing.

candidates.summary: Parallel input-section path skips relocation apply when a section has empty data, so a SHT_PROGBITS section with relocs but zero-length data would not be patched
candidates.evidence: Line 342: `if data_len == 0 || file_off + data_len > buf_len { continue; }` short-circuits before the reloc-apply block (lines 354-361). A degenerate-but-legal input section with `data.len()==0` yet a non-empty `relocs` list would have its relocations silently skipped. The legacy serial path (write_section_data) has the same shape, so the two paths agree, but neither applies relocations for empty-data sections. This is an edge case (most relocated sections carry bytes), but it is a silent divergence from 'apply all relocations against final virtual addresses' (module doc, step 4).

candidates.summary: ws-deque is an absolute local-path dependency, making the workspace unbuildable on any machine other than the author's and breaking reproducibility/portability
candidates.evidence: `ws-deque = { path = "/home/cole/RustProjects/ws-deque" }` (workspace dep, line 23) is an absolute filesystem path baked into the workspace manifest and consumed by peony-cache, peony-emit, and peony-layout. On any clone where that exact path does not exist, `cargo build` fails to resolve the dependency — CI, other developers, and release builds cannot reproduce the build. There is no version/registry/git fallback. This is a hard portability regression for the whole workspace, not just an out-of-scope note.

findings.summary: Incremental cache key ignores linker arguments — changing args (e.g. --gc-sections, --entry) reuses a stale binary
findings.evidence: Confirmed against source. try_reuse(output: &Path, inputs: &[PathBuf]) (lib.rs:287) accepts no argument representation and compares only manifest.version (301), per-input path+fingerprint pairs (309-317), and the output fingerprint (320-322). record_link_with_sections() computes epoch_key = compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0) (line 352) with args_hash HARDCODED to 0, and record_link() — the path the driver uses (main.rs:252) — funnels through it. The driver calls try_reuse(&args.output, &inputs) at main.rs:167 where `inputs` are only resolved positional file paths (main.rs:161-162, and parse_args sends only positional args to a.inputs at main.rs:132). Flags --gc-sections (main.rs:112) and --entry/-e (main.rs:106,130) change link output but never alter `inputs`. So `peony foo.o -o a.out --incremental` followed by `peony foo.o -o a.out --gc-sections --entry main --incremental` matches all checked fields (same input file, same fingerprint, unchanged output fingerprint) → try_reuse returns true → the previous binary, which does not reflect the new args, is silently reused. Additionally, try_reuse never even loads/compares manifest.epoch_key, so the advertised epoch-key fast path (doc lines 6-10) is doubly non-functional w.r.t. args. (Note: doc says SHA-256 but code uses FNV-1a; immaterial to this bug.)
findings.fix: Plumb the linker arguments into the cache. Capture an args hash in the driver (hash_args() already exists at lib.rs:240, computed over the argv minus volatile/irrelevant flags), pass it to record_link/record_link_with_sections instead of the hardcoded 0, and add an args_hash parameter to try_reuse so it rejects reuse when the stored manifest's args hash differs. Equivalently, store args_hash in the Manifest and have try_reuse compare it (and/or actually compare manifest.epoch_key recomputed with the current args_hash) before returning true.
findings.reason: The candidate is concrete and reproducible from the code as written. try_reuse compares only inputs and output fingerprints and never receives or checks any args representation; record_link_with_sections hardcodes args_hash=0; the driver passes only resolved input paths; and behavior-changing flags like --gc-sections/--entry do not alter `inputs`. The reproduction scenario therefore yields a false cache hit and reuse of a stale binary — a genuine high-severity incremental-correctness bug. The candidate's only minor imprecision (citing the doc's SHA-256 wording, and framing the defect solely around args_hash=0 when try_reuse also never compares the stored epoch_key) does not undermine the conclusion. Line 287 is the correct anchor for the defective reuse check.

findings.summary: Relocation writers index buf[off..off+N] using an unvalidated reloc offset, panicking (slice out of bounds) on malformed/out-of-range relocation offsets in input object files.
findings.evidence: Confirmed in source. write_u64 (peony-reloc/src/lib.rs:400 `buf[off..off+8]`), write_i32 (:415 `buf[off..off+4]`), write_u32 (:432), write_u16 (:440) and write_u8 (:448 `buf[off]`) only validate value width/overflow — never `off+N <= buf.len()`. `off` is `reloc.offset as usize`, set in apply_reloc (:292) and consumed in patch_buf (:300). reloc.offset is the raw ELF r_offset: peony-object/src/lib.rs:370-375 pushes `offset` straight from `object::section.relocations()` with no validation against the section's data size. In peony-emit, the slice passed to apply_reloc is exactly `data_len = isec.data.len()` bytes (lib.rs:341-350); the only guard there is `file_off + data_len > buf_len` (:342) for the section copy, not the reloc offset within the section. apply_reloc runs inside `std::thread::scope`/`scope.spawn` (peony-emit lib.rs:302/311), so a panic is re-raised on scope join and aborts the link after the output buffer is already created. By contrast the legacy serial writers guard explicitly: write_bytes (lib.rs:494 `if end <= buf.len()`), write_symtab (:468 `if off + 24 > buf.len() break`), GOT writer (:231 `if off + 8 <= buf.len()`). I reproduced the panic: `buf[12..20]` on a 16-byte buffer panics with slice-index out of bounds (catch_unwind confirms panicked=true).
findings.fix: In each writer (or once in patch_buf before dispatch), bounds-check the write window before indexing, e.g. add `if off.checked_add(8).map_or(true, |e| e > buf.len()) { return Err(...truncated/out-of-range reloc...); }` (analogously +4/+2/+1 for the narrower writers), returning a RelocError instead of indexing out of bounds. This mirrors the `if off + N <= buf.len()` guards already used by write_bytes/write_symtab/the GOT writer in peony-emit, and turns a panic-and-abort into a recoverable, reported link error.
findings.reason: Verified concrete and reproducible. The writers (peony-reloc/src/lib.rs:399-449) index buf[off..off+N] with no bounds check; off derives from the raw, unvalidated ELF r_offset (peony-object/src/lib.rs:370) and the buffer is exactly the section's data length (peony-emit/src/lib.rs:341-350). No upstream validation exists on the offset path, so a crafted/malformed object with a reloc offset near or past the section end (off+N > data_len) triggers a slice-index panic, which re-raises out of std::thread::scope and aborts the link with the output partially written. The asymmetry with the guarded legacy writers (write_bytes/write_symtab/GOT) reinforces this is an oversight. I empirically reproduced the panic. Severity medium is apt: a linker processes untrusted third-party objects/archives, but the impact is a safe panic/DoS with partial output (the bounds check is present at the Rust level, hence panic not UB), not memory corruption. The file is within the target edited set.

findings.summary: section_references_moved uses a hardcoded section-name heuristic that mis-colors output sections, producing both false-Green (unsound skip of re-emit) and false-Red (lost incremental reuse) results, contradicting the function's own documented red-green contract.
findings.evidence: peony-cache/src/lib.rs:441 implements `section_references_moved` as `!moved.is_empty() && (section_name == ".text" || section_name == ".data" || section_name == ".rodata")`. compute_red_green (line 422) colors a section Green only when `prev_fp == *fp && !section_references_moved(name, &moved)`. (1) FALSE GREEN: any reloc-bearing output section not named exactly .text/.data/.rodata (e.g. .init_array, .fini_array, .got, .data.rel.ro, .tdata, .tbss) returns false, so an unchanged fingerprint yields Green = "skip re-emit" (doc line 91) even if it references a moved symbol — a stale/incorrect binary, directly violating the function doc (lines 370-371: Green requires "no relocation target moved"). (2) FALSE RED: when any symbol moved, every .text/.data/.rodata section returns true regardless of whether it actually references the moved symbol, forcing Red and defeating incremental reuse. The crate already defines RelocReverseIndex (lines 124-185) with iter_relocs(symbol_id) — the exact mechanism the comments (lines 432-440: "Heuristic", "conservative approximation", "A production implementation would use the relocation reverse index") say should be used but is not. Confirmed only test callers (peony/tests/algorithms.rs:280,311); no driver wiring, limiting present impact.
findings.fix: Replace the name-based heuristic with a real reloc lookup: map each output section to the input relocations it carries, resolve each reloc's target symbol id, and query RelocReverseIndex / the moved set so the function returns true iff this specific section has a relocation against a symbol in `moved`. Until that wiring exists, make the fallback conservative-correct rather than name-gated — e.g. return `!moved.is_empty()` (treat every section as potentially affected when any symbol moved) so it never produces a false Green, and add a test exercising a non-.text reloc-bearing section (e.g. .init_array / .data.rel.ro) to lock the behavior.
findings.reason: All candidate claims verified against the source. Line 441 matches the cited code verbatim. The Green path (line 422) plus the SectionColor::Green doc ("skip re-emit", line 91) and the compute_red_green doc (lines 370-371 requiring "no relocation target moved" for Green) confirm both the false-Green soundness bug and the false-Red incrementality regression. RelocReverseIndex with iter_relocs exists in the same crate (lines 124-185), confirming the intended-but-unused mechanism. grep confirms compute_red_green/section_references_moved have only test callers, supporting the "limited present-day impact" caveat. The file is in the target edited-files list and, because the repo has no commits yet, the entire working tree constitutes the diff under review. The issue is concrete, reproducible from the code, and worth reporting; medium severity is appropriate. Confidence raised from the candidate's 0.78 to 0.9 after direct confirmation.

findings.summary: Work-stealing loop collapses Steal::Retry into the no-op Empty arm, so a thief abandons a victim that demonstrably still has stealable work, degrading load balancing (not correctness).
findings.evidence: peony-emit/src/lib.rs:321-324 matches `s.steal()` as `Steal::Success(v) => {..}` and `Steal::Empty | Steal::Retry => {}`. The same pattern appears in peony-layout/src/lib.rs:1207-1211. The ws-deque crate (this workspace's /home/cole/RustProjects/ws-deque/src/lib.rs:121-124) documents these as DISTINCT outcomes: `Empty` = "deque was observed empty"; `Retry` = "Lost a race with another thief or an emptying pop; the caller should retry" (i.e. the deque is non-empty). The crate even provides `is_retry()` (lib.rs:148-152) "so retry-loops port directly." By treating Retry as a no-op, the thief moves on (next stealer / spin_loop at line 330) instead of re-attempting the victim that still has work. Items are seeded round-robin (lines 296-298), so victims genuinely hold work, making lost-race Retry outcomes realistic. The candidate's claim that no work is lost is correct: each Worker drains its own deque via pop() (line 316) before stealing, and `done` is set (line 365) only after all threads are spawned, so every seeded item is still processed by its owner. The impact is purely reduced work-stealing effectiveness, not incorrectness.
findings.fix: Handle Retry distinctly by re-attempting the same stealer until it yields Success or Empty, e.g. `loop { match s.steal() { Steal::Success(v) => { stolen = Some(v); break 'outer; } Steal::Retry => continue, Steal::Empty => break, } }`, or use the provided `is_retry()` helper. This preserves the work-stealing balance the design (QUAD §5.1) relies on. Apply the same change to the identical loop in peony-layout/src/lib.rs:1207-1211.
findings.reason: All factual claims in the candidate were verified against the repository. The code at peony-emit/src/lib.rs:321-324 (and the duplicate at peony-layout/src/lib.rs:1207-1211) does collapse Steal::Retry into the no-op arm. The ws-deque source confirms Retry and Empty are semantically distinct (Retry = non-empty, lost a race, should retry), and the crate exposes is_retry() precisely so callers don't conflate them. The candidate correctly notes this is not a correctness/work-loss bug (each worker drains its own queue via pop before stealing), only a load-balancing/performance degradation, which justifies the low severity. The issue is concrete, reproducible directly from the code, and a legitimate (if minor) review comment with an actionable fix. Line reference 323 lands within the cited match block.

findings.summary: On-disk manifest is bincode-decoded with the limit-less `standard()` config, so a corrupt/hostile length field can trigger an unbounded eager allocation (abort/OOM) before validation.
findings.evidence: try_reuse (lib.rs:296-300) and compute_red_green (392-401) both decode manifest.bin via `bincode::serde::decode_from_slice(&bytes, bincode::config::standard())`. Verified against bincode 2.0.1 (Cargo.lock:77-80) + serde 1.0.228: `standard()` resolves to `Configuration<..., NoLimit>` (no byte limit). The OOM path is the String fields inside `Manifest.inputs: Vec<(String, Fingerprint)>` — NOT the outer Vec. The outer Vec uses bincode's serde `deserialize_seq`, and serde's `Vec::deserialize` clamps pre-allocation via `size_hint::cautious` (MAX_PREALLOC_BYTES = 1 MiB), so a giant outer-Vec length only fails mid-read. But each `String` routes through bincode's `deserialize_string` -> native `Vec<u8>::decode`, which does `let mut vec = alloc::vec![0u8; len];` with `len = decode_slice_len()` read straight from the untrusted file. The protective `claim_container_read` is a no-op under NoLimit (de/mod.rs:182-191: `if LIMIT.is_some() {...} else { Ok(()) }`). So a manifest with a String length of ~2^60 forces an immediate `vec![0u8; 2^60]` -> allocation failure/abort, instead of the intended graceful fall-back to a full link. Two inaccuracies in the original candidate: (1) the 2^60 example was attributed to a `Vec` length, but the unbounded path is the inner `String`; the outer Vec is in fact cautious-clamped; (2) the claim that 'QUAD/SPEC notes call out untrusted-input deserialization' is unsupported — no such note exists in research/SPEC.md or the digests.
findings.fix: Decode with an explicit byte limit so `claim_container_read` enforces bounds, e.g. `bincode::config::standard().with_limit::<MAX>()` where MAX is derived from the actual file size (`bytes.len()`), since the decoded data can never legitimately exceed the encoded length. This converts a hostile/corrupt length into a clean `DecodeError::LimitExceeded`, which the existing `Err(_) => Ok(false)/all-Red` arms already handle gracefully. Apply at both lib.rs:297 and lib.rs:393.
findings.reason: Confirmed concrete and reproducible from the code. The repo (no commits yet; all files untracked, so the 'diff' is the whole new file) contains the cited decode sites verbatim. Tracing bincode 2.0.1 + serde 1.0.228 source confirms `standard()` = NoLimit and that `claim_container_read` is a no-op without a limit, so native `Vec<u8>`/`String` decode performs `vec![0u8; len]` with an attacker/corruption-controlled `len` -> unbounded allocation/abort before any version or fingerprint validation. Marked valid, severity low: the manifest lives in a user-local `<output>.incr/` dir writable only by the invoking user, so this is a robustness/hardening defect (corrupt cache aborts the linker rather than falling back to a full link) rather than a real trust-boundary breach. Confidence raised above the candidate's 0.45 because the mechanism is verified end-to-end, but two of the candidate's specifics were wrong (the dominant alloc path is the inner String, not the outer Vec which is cautious-clamped; and the cited SPEC/QUAD note does not exist), which I corrected in the evidence/fix.

findings.summary: Documented epoch-key fast path is never executed; try_reuse always re-fingerprints every input file
findings.evidence: Module-level docs (lines 5–10) and the try_reuse doc comment (lines 283–286) promise an O(1) epoch-gated fast path: "compare the epoch key. If it matches, skip reading any input file." In practice, try_reuse (lines 287–324) never reads or compares manifest.epoch_key. After deserialising the manifest it goes straight to calling Fingerprint::of_file(cur) on every input (line 313) and Fingerprint::of_file(output) on the output (line 320), unconditionally reading and hashing every byte. The epoch_key field is written in record_link_with_sections (line 355) but the only code that reads it is in the test suite (lines 546–547). No production code path computes the current epoch key and compares it to the stored one before falling through to the full fingerprint loop.
findings.fix: Implement the fast path at the top of try_reuse: call compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0) (or pass args_hash through the API), compare the result to manifest.epoch_key, and if they match verify only the output fingerprint—skipping the per-input Fingerprint::of_file calls. If the epoch key API signature needs to change to accept an args_hash, update record_link/record_link_with_sections accordingly (currently they pass 0 for args_hash). Alternatively, remove the fast-path promise from the documentation to match the current behavior.
findings.reason: The claim is fully reproducible from the source. try_reuse at lines 287–324 contains no reference to manifest.epoch_key whatsoever; searching the file confirms epoch_key is used only in the Manifest struct definition (line 261), written at line 355, and read exclusively in two test functions (lines 546–547). The module and function docs explicitly promise a fast path that skips input reads when the epoch key matches, making this a concrete doc-impl mismatch. Severity is low because the only consequence is a missed performance optimisation; correctness is unaffected.

findings.summary: overwrite_in_place test relinks identical inputs, so it cannot catch stale-byte corruption from the non-zeroing overwrite-in-place fast path
findings.evidence: Verified against the repo. `overwrite_in_place_produces_identical_output` (peony/tests/algorithms.rs:162-188) links the SAME object twice (obj.clone() at lines 177 and 182), so `bytes1 == bytes2` is trivially true regardless of correctness. In peony-emit/src/lib.rs the buffer is zeroed ONLY in the `!can_overwrite` branch (lines 117-119: `if !can_overwrite { mmap.iter_mut().for_each(|b| *b = 0); }`); the overwrite-in-place branch (can_overwrite==true when existing file size matches, lines 91-99) opens the existing file with O_RDWR and no truncate, reusing its prior contents WITHOUT zeroing. write_section_data_parallel (lines 270-352) writes only each section's `isec.data` range via copy_from_slice; alignment-gap/padding bytes — produced by the many `align_up(...)` calls in peony-layout/src/lib.rs (e.g. line 634 `align_up(file_content_end, 8)`) — plus BSS file-image regions are never rewritten and depend solely on zero-init. emit_full runs this path UNCONDITIONALLY (peony/src/main.rs:248), not just under --incremental; the cache only short-circuits when inputs are unchanged (main.rs:166). The other relink tests (incremental_partial_change in more_cases.rs:193, incremental_reuse_and_relink in link_and_run.rs:114) change a same-sized immediate operand so they DO hit the overwrite path, but they validate only via run() execution and never byte-diff against a fresh truncating link, so stale gap/padding bytes off the executed code path go undetected.
findings.fix: Add a test that (a) links input A, (b) relinks DIFFERENT content B that produces the SAME file_size (forcing can_overwrite==true), then (c) links B into a fresh path with a pre-truncated/removed output (forcing the zeroing path) and asserts the two B binaries are byte-identical. This pins down that the overwrite-in-place path leaves no stale gap/padding/BSS-image bytes. Alternatively, unconditionally zero the buffer (or explicitly zero all non-section/gap ranges) in emit_full even on the can_overwrite path to eliminate the stale-byte hazard, and keep a regression test for it.
findings.reason: All concrete claims in the candidate were verified against the source: line 162 links the same object twice (trivial bytes1==bytes2); zeroing is gated on `!can_overwrite` at peony-emit/src/lib.rs:117-119; the overwrite-in-place branch (lines 91-99) reuses file contents without zeroing; section-data copy writes only isec.data ranges so align_up-induced gap/padding and BSS-image bytes rely on zero-init; emit_full runs this path unconditionally (main.rs:248); and no existing test byte-diffs a same-size different-content relink against a fresh truncating link. This is a genuine, reproducible-from-code coverage gap that gives false confidence in overwrite-in-place correctness. I lowered severity from the candidate's 'high' to 'medium' because it is a latent test-quality/missing-test issue rather than a demonstrated shipping bug — a real stale-byte failure requires a same-size-but-different-padding relink scenario, which is plausible but not the common case.

findings.summary: No test exercises the relocation overflow / signed-unsigned range-check logic in write_i32/write_u32/write_u16/write_u8; the only corpus test for it is #[ignore]d and empty.
findings.evidence: Verified in the repo: peony-reloc has zero #[test] functions and no tests module (grep -rc '#[test]' peony-reloc/ => ZERO). The width-checked writers carry subtle range logic that nothing exercises: write_i32 (lib.rs:411-413) checks `let v = val as i32; if v as i64 != val { return Err(overflow(...)) }`; write_u32 (lib.rs:419-434) accepts a value if EITHER the zero-extended u32 round-trips OR the sign-extended i32 round-trips: `if (u as u32) as u64 != u && (val as i32) as i64 != val { return Err(...) }`; write_u16 (436-442) and write_u8 (444-449) use the same dual i/u round-trip pattern. The overflow() helper and RelocError::Overflow variant exist (lib.rs:36, 452-453) but are never asserted by any test. The only x86_64 overflow corpus test, mold_arch_x86_64_reloc_overflow (peony/tests/mold_corpus.rs:492-495), is `#[ignore = \"needs unsupported feature\"]` with an empty body. Existing relocation integration tests in peony/tests/relocations.rs (abs64_and_pc32, pc64_cross_object, byte_reloc_r8 with small=7, combined_relocations) only drive the success path and assert exit codes; none pushes a value past the i32/u32/u16/u8 range to hit the error branches. A regression flipping && to || or dropping a branch in these comparisons would silently truncate an out-of-range PC32/PLT32/GOT/R32 value into the output with no test failing.
findings.fix: Add unit tests in peony-reloc (e.g. a #[cfg(test)] mod) that call write_i32/write_u32/write_u16/write_u8 directly with both in-range boundary values (e.g. i32::MIN/MAX, u32::MAX, -1) and out-of-range values, asserting Ok(()) writes the expected little-endian bytes and that out-of-range inputs return RelocError::Overflow. In particular, cover write_u32's dual-acceptance contract: a sign-extended value like -1 (0xFFFF_FFFF_FFFF_FFFF) should succeed, while 0x1_0000_0000 should overflow. This pins the range-check semantics so a flipped comparison is caught.
findings.reason: The candidate's substantive claims are all verified against the code: peony-reloc has no tests, write_i32/write_u32/write_u16/write_u8 contain the exact subtle range logic described, the overflow error path is never asserted, and the only x86_64 overflow corpus test is #[ignore]d/empty. The existing relocations.rs tests cover only success paths. This is a genuine, reproducible coverage gap in code where a silent regression would miscompile binaries, so it is worth reporting. Minor inaccuracies do not undermine it: the cited corpus line range was 491-495 (actual 492-495) and the ignore reason is \"needs unsupported feature\". I lowered severity from the candidate's 'high' to 'medium' because this is a test-coverage gap rather than a live defect, and I note the stated target (current git diff) is technically empty — the repository has no commits and all files are untracked — so the finding applies to the codebase as-is rather than to a focused change; peony-reloc/src/lib.rs is nonetheless in the listed target files, so the candidate is in scope.

findings.summary: Red-green coloring tests never pass a non-empty moved_symbols set, so the moved-symbol→Red path (the core Theorem 6.1 mechanism) is entirely untested.
findings.evidence: Both red_green tests call compute_red_green(&fake_output, &current, &[]) with an empty moved_symbols slice: algorithms.rs:280 (red_green_all_green_when_nothing_changes, fn at line 246) and algorithms.rs:311 (red_green_changed_section_is_red, fn at line 288). A grep across the whole repo confirms these are the ONLY call sites of compute_red_green in tests, and section_references_moved (peony-cache/src/lib.rs:435) is called only from compute_red_green at lib.rs:422. With an empty slice, section_references_moved returns false immediately (lib.rs:436-438), so the guard `!section_references_moved(name, &moved)` at line 422 and the heuristic that forces .text/.data/.rodata to Red (lib.rs:441) are never exercised. There are no unit tests inside peony-cache/src/lib.rs covering this path either. A regression that disables moved-symbol invalidation (e.g. section_references_moved always returns false) would still pass both tests, yet would mark a fingerprint-identical section Green and skip re-emit even though a relocation target moved — the exact unsound case the cited Theorem 6.1 guards against.
findings.fix: Add a test that records a link, then calls compute_red_green with a non-empty moved_symbols slice and a fingerprint-identical current section (e.g. ".text") and asserts the section is colored Red despite the unchanged fingerprint. Also add a negative case where the moved symbol's section is not in the heuristic set to confirm it stays Green, locking in the moved-symbol → Red behavior.
findings.reason: Verified against the repository: both compute_red_green test call sites (algorithms.rs:280 and :311) pass an empty &[] for moved_symbols, and a full-repo grep confirms no test anywhere supplies a non-empty moved_symbols slice. section_references_moved (peony-cache/src/lib.rs:435) is reachable only through compute_red_green's line-422 guard, so the moved-symbol→Red branch — the safety mechanism the code's own doc comments tie to Theorem 6.1 — has zero coverage. The finding is concrete, reproducible, and worth reporting. I lowered severity from 'high' to 'medium' because this is a test-coverage gap rather than a live defect in shipping logic; the production note at lib.rs:434 also acknowledges section_references_moved is a heuristic placeholder, but the gap is still legitimate. The candidate's line:246 anchor is the test fn declaration, which is acceptable.

findings.summary: s3gc_chain_multiple_bfs_levels only asserts exit code 0 and never verifies dead sections are dropped, so an over-keeping GC regression is undetectable
findings.evidence: Confirmed by reading the file. The test's docstring (lines 67-68) claims "All of B, C, D must be live; any extra sections beyond these four must be dead." The fixture (lines 73-109) defines a live chain fn_a→fn_b→fn_c→fn_d (rooted at _start) plus an unreferenced dead_fn in section .text.dead (lines 97-100). The ONLY assertion in the body is `assert_eq!(run(&out), 0, "chain GC binary must run and exit 0")` at line 113. The run() helper (common/mod.rs ~line 100) merely executes the binary and returns its exit code — it does no section/symbol inspection. _start (lines 104-108) calls fn_a then exits 0 via `xor %rdi,%rdi; syscall` REGARDLESS of GC behavior, and dead_fn is just a `ret`, so keeping it has no runtime effect. Consequently a GC bug that keeps ALL sections (or disables GC entirely) still exits 0 and passes. Unlike the sibling test s3gc_live_set_is_correct_and_smaller (lines 22-65) which links with and without --gc-sections and asserts `sz_gc < sz_no_gc`, this test does no size comparison and no readelf/symbol inspection, so its docstring claim that the live set is exactly correct is unverified.
findings.fix: Strengthen the test to verify dead-code elimination actually happened: link the same fixture with and without --gc-sections and assert the GC'd binary is strictly smaller (mirroring s3gc_live_set_is_correct_and_smaller), and/or inspect the GC'd output with readelf to assert the .text.dead section and the dead_fn symbol are absent while fn_a..fn_d remain present.
findings.reason: All candidate claims verified directly against the source. The test at line 70 asserts only `assert_eq!(run(&out), 0)` at line 113, performs no size comparison or section/symbol inspection, and its fixture's dead_fn (.text.dead) is unreferenced. Because _start exits 0 independent of GC outcome and dead_fn is a no-op ret, an over-keeping GC regression (or fully disabled GC) would still pass, leaving the docstring's "live set is exactly correct / extra sections must be dead" contract unverified. This is a concrete, reproducible weak-test defect worth reporting; medium severity is appropriate since it degrades regression coverage rather than being a production bug. (Note: this is a new untracked repo with no commits, so the "diff" is the entire new file — the targeted code is present and the finding stands.)

findings.summary: In write_section_data_parallel, every worker thread acquires the shared error_slot Mutex on every iteration of the inner work-stealing loop just to check is_some(), serializing all threads on a single futex even on the common no-error path; the read only needs to observe an error, so an AtomicBool sentinel (as already used for `done`) would make the hot path lock-free.
findings.evidence: Confirmed by reading the source (no git HEAD exists; files are untracked, so the diff target is the current file contents). Line 314 reads exactly `if error_slot.lock().unwrap().is_some() { break; }` at the top of the `loop {` (line 312) inside fn write_section_data_parallel (line 203). error_slot is `Arc<std::sync::Mutex<Option<peony_reloc::RelocError>>>` (lines 289-290). The mutex is acquired and released unconditionally on every loop iteration, before the worker.pop()/steal at lines 316-334, i.e. once per section per thread on both error and no-error paths. std::sync::Mutex on Linux is a futex, so this is genuine cross-thread contention, not a trivial spinlock. The error WRITE path correctly locks at line 357 (`*error_slot.lock().unwrap() = Some(e)`), but the READ at line 314 needs no mutual exclusion. AtomicBool and Ordering are already imported (line 18) and the early-exit `done` flag already uses AtomicBool (line 300) with Ordering::Acquire/Release (lines 329, 365), proving the lock-free sentinel pattern is idiomatic here. Strong corroboration: the near-identical work-stealing loop in peony-layout/src/lib.rs (lines 1199-1239) accumulates into a per-thread local_out and locks `results` only once at the end (line 1238), with NO per-iteration mutex in its loop — confirming line 314 is an anomaly versus the codebase's own convention.
findings.fix: Replace the per-iteration mutex read at line 314 with an AtomicBool sentinel. Add `let error_flag = Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(false));` alongside error_slot, clone it into each thread, and change line 314 to `if error_flag.load(Ordering::Acquire) { break; }`. On the error path (line 357), set the concrete error under the mutex once and then signal: `*error_slot.lock().unwrap() = Some(e); error_flag.store(true, Ordering::Release); break;`. This keeps first-error-wins semantics and exact-error propagation (line 368 still reads error_slot after the scope) while making the hot read path lock-free.
findings.reason: Every claim in the candidate verifies against the source: line 314 content, error_slot type (289-290), per-iteration unconditional lock in the hot loop, the write-path lock at 357, the pre-existing AtomicBool `done` sentinel at 300, and the imports at line 18. The contrast with the sibling work-stealing loop in peony-layout (1199-1239), which uses per-thread accumulation and locks only once, independently confirms the pattern is an anomaly and the proposed fix is the codebase's own idiom. The bug is real (genuine futex contention across N threads, M sections), the proposed AtomicBool fix is correct and preserves error semantics, and 'medium' severity is appropriate since it is a performance/scalability defect rather than a correctness bug. File is within the sweep target set. Knocked slightly below certainty only because the absolute throughput impact depends on workload size and steal-loop dynamics, but the defect and fix are unambiguous.

findings.summary: WorkItem tuple's `data_len` field (index 1) is always hardcoded to `0usize` and silently discarded; the type comment falsely claims it holds the section data length, creating a latent maintainer trap
findings.evidence: Line 266–267 defines the comment `// Work item: (file_offset, data_len, section_va, object_id, section_index)` and `type WorkItem = (usize, usize, u64, usize, usize)`. Line 275 constructs every work item as `(file_off, 0usize, section_va, c.object_id, c.section_index)` — the second field is always the literal `0`. Line 336 immediately discards it: `let (file_off, _, section_va, obj_id, sec_idx) = item;`. Line 341 then re-derives the actual length as `let data_len = isec.data.len();`. The stored `0` is never read. Meanwhile `SectionContribution.size: u64` (peony-layout/src/lib.rs:60) is available at the call site as `c.size`, making `c.size as usize` the natural value to store — but it is not used. The gap between the comment's promise and the always-zero value stored is invisible without reading the consumer, and unnamed tuple fields make it easy to overlook.
findings.fix: Either (a) populate the field with the actual value — `(file_off, c.size as usize, section_va, c.object_id, c.section_index)` — and use it at line 342 as a pre-filter (`if stored_len == 0 || file_off + stored_len > buf_len { continue; }`) instead of re-deriving from `isec.data.len()`; or (b) remove the field entirely from the tuple type, the comment, and all construction/destructuring sites if it serves no purpose. Option (a) preserves the intent of the comment; option (b) eliminates dead weight. Either way, a named struct (or a local struct/record) would make future confusion impossible.
findings.reason: All three claims verified in the source: (1) the comment names the field `data_len`, (2) line 275 stores `0usize` in that slot unconditionally, (3) line 336 discards it with `_`, and (4) line 341 recomputes the length from `isec.data.len()`. The fix candidate (`c.size as usize`) is structurally valid — `SectionContribution.size: u64` exists and is in scope at line 272–275. No correctness bug exists today because the value is always re-derived before use, so severity is correctly low, but the misleading contract is a real maintainability risk.

findings.summary: PLT stub RIP-relative displacement is narrowed i64→i32 with an unchecked `as i32` cast and written directly into the stub, so a >2 GiB .plt/.got.plt separation would silently emit a wrong jmp target — unlike the round-trip-checked write_i32 in peony-reloc. Genuine robustness defect, but the candidate's stated trigger (large --base-address) is factually wrong.
findings.evidence: Confirmed at peony-layout/src/lib.rs:847: `let disp = (slot_va as i64 - (stub_va as i64 + 6)) as i32;` followed by line 851 `stub[2..6].copy_from_slice(&disp.to_le_bytes())`, writing the truncated disp32 directly into the FF 25 (jmp *slot(%rip)) stub with no overflow check and no reloc-record path. By contrast peony-reloc/src/lib.rs:411-413 (write_i32) performs `let v = val as i32; if v as i64 != val { return Err(overflow(...)) }`. The arithmetic claim is correct: a displacement ≥2 GiB truncates to a wrong target. HOWEVER the candidate's feasibility reasoning is wrong: layout is strictly sequential from a single `base` (lines 593-617) — `.plt` is placed in the RX group (line 510) and `.got.plt` in the RW group (line 521), both growing from the same `base`. Thus `gotplt_base - plt_base` is bounded by intervening code/data size + page alignment, NOT by base_address (which shifts both equally and leaves their difference unchanged); the PIE path uses base 0. So the claimed trigger via large `--base-address` cannot occur; only a single output with >2 GiB of RX between .plt and .got.plt (a degenerate, never-realistically-produced binary) could overflow.
findings.fix: Mirror the reloc writers' guard at line 847: compute `let disp64 = slot_va as i64 - (stub_va as i64 + 6); let disp = i32::try_from(disp64).map_err(|_| /* layout error: PLT displacement out of range */)?;` (or an explicit round-trip `disp as i64 != disp64` check) and propagate an error instead of silently truncating, making the layout phase consistent with peony-reloc::write_i32.
findings.reason: The core defect is real and precisely located: line 847 truncates the i64 PLT displacement to i32 with no overflow check and writes it directly (line 851), bypassing the round-trip-checked write_i32 in peony-reloc (lines 411-413) — a genuine inconsistency and a silent-wrong-output risk worth fixing. I confirmed every code-level claim by reading the source. However the candidate's exploitability rationale is materially incorrect: it attributes the >2 GiB separation to large `--base-address`, but base_address shifts `.plt` and `.got.plt` by the same offset (sequential layout from a single base, lines 593-617), so it cannot widen their gap; the gap is bounded by binary size. The only real trigger is a degenerate >2 GiB-of-code binary this minimal static linker would never produce. So the finding is valid as a low-severity code-quality/robustness issue, but its severity and triggering analysis are overstated — hence reduced confidence.

findings.summary: parallel_emit_is_deterministic asserts only that 8 concurrent links produce byte-identical output; it never executes any binary, so a deterministic miscompile producing 8 identical-but-corrupt binaries passes silently.
findings.evidence: Verified against source. peony/tests/algorithms.rs:121-155: the test spawns 8 threads, each calls link() then fs::read(&out), collects the byte vectors, and at lines 152-154 asserts only assert_eq!(first, r, ...) — pure byte-equality across the 8 outputs. There is no run() call anywhere in the test (grep of run( in algorithms.rs returns lines 58,113,187,376,382 — none inside 121-155). The fixture (lines 125-138) deterministically exits with code 99: `leaq x(%rip),%rax; movq $99,(%rax)` stores 99 into the .data word x, then `mov $60,%rax` (sys_exit), `movq x(%rip),%rdi` loads 99 into rdi, `syscall` → exit(99). The run() helper exists at peony/tests/common/mod.rs:100-106 and returns the process exit code as i32, so the candidate's proposed correctness check assert_eq!(run(&...), 99) is exactly right. The sibling test overwrite_in_place_produces_identical_output (lines 162-188) demonstrates the correct pattern: it byte-compares AND asserts assert_eq!(run(&out), 7) at line 187. Thus a deterministic relocation bug (double-applied RIP-relative PC32 patch, wrong section offset, or wrong ELF entry point) would yield 8 byte-identical corrupt binaries and pass all assertions undetected. Secondary: the comment at line 124 claims 'weak symbols' but the assembly has no .weak directives — only .globl x and .globl _start with two RIP-relative references — so the comment overstates the fixture's coverage.
findings.fix: Capture each thread's output path and, after the byte-equality loop, execute one of the resulting binaries to confirm correctness, e.g. assert_eq!(run(&dir.join("det_0.out")), 99, "deterministic output must also be correct, not just identical"). Optionally also fix the misleading line-124 comment (no weak symbols / GOT/PLT relocs are present) or add a .weak symbol and a PLT reference to make the fixture match the comment.
findings.reason: Every technical claim in the candidate checks out against the source: the test (algorithms.rs:121-155) verifies byte-identity across 8 concurrent links but never calls run(); the fixture provably exits with code 99 via the RIP-relative load/store sequence; run() exists in common/mod.rs and returns the exit code; and the sibling overwrite_in_place test (line 187) establishes the correct pattern of pairing determinism with an execution check. The weakness is real — a deterministic miscompile would produce 8 identical corrupt binaries that pass all assertions — and the proposed fix is concrete and correct. The 'low' severity is appropriate: this is a test-quality gap, not a shipping bug, and the test's primary stated purpose (QUAD Theorem 12.1 determinism) is in fact exercised; a gross miscompile would likely also be caught by the many other run()-checking tests. The minor comment inaccuracy about 'weak symbols' is also correctly noted. Confidence is high; capped slightly because the value of the added assertion is incremental given broad run() coverage elsewhere in the suite.

dismissed.summary: Candidate incorrectly claims imports are excluded from GOT allocation by `is_defined()`
dismissed.evidence: The candidate claims that `res.is_defined() == false` for dynamic imports, so the guard `needs_got(reloc.r_type) && res.is_defined()` at line 182 would silently skip GOT-slot creation for imported symbols. In reality, `SymbolResolution::is_defined()` (peony-symbols/src/lib.rs line 139-141) is defined as `self.defined_in.is_some() || self.import`. When a symbol is a shared-library import, `self.import == true`, so `is_defined()` returns `true`. The GOT-slot allocation path at line 182-183 therefore fires for imports with GOTPCREL/GOT32 relocations. Furthermore, the layout in peony-layout/src/lib.rs lines 331-339 counts `n_glob_dat` by iterating over `got_syms` and filtering those where `r.import`, which correctly covers exactly these import slots to emit GLOB_DAT relocs. The entire pipeline is consistent and correct for the described scenario.
dismissed.fix: No fix needed. The logic is correct as written.
dismissed.reason: The candidate's premise is factually incorrect. `SymbolResolution::is_defined()` explicitly returns `true` for imports (`self.defined_in.is_some() || self.import`), so the guard on line 182 does allocate GOT slots for dynamic imports referenced via GOTPCREL/GOT32. The layout layer then correctly counts those import-flagged GOT slots to produce GLOB_DAT entries. There is no bug in the described code path.

dismissed.summary: Claimed missing cache-invalidation test actually exists in sibling integration-test files; the asserted coverage gap is false.
dismissed.evidence: The candidate claims no test exercises try_reuse() returning false (modify an input, relink, assert output changed and runs correctly). Two tests in the same peony/tests/ crate do exactly that: (1) incremental_reuse_and_relink (peony/tests/link_and_run.rs:114-140) links with --incremental, modifies the input object ($42 -> $7), relinks, asserts stderr does NOT contain 'reused cached output' ('should have relinked after a change'), and asserts run(&exe) == 7 (correct new output). (2) incremental_partial_change (peony/tests/more_cases.rs:193-215) changes one of two inputs (42 -> 7), relinks, asserts not reused, and asserts run(&exe) == 7. Both assertions would fail if try_reuse() in peony-cache/src/lib.rs:287-324 returned true too eagerly after a real source change, directly catching the 'silent incremental miscompile' regression the candidate worried about.
dismissed.fix: No fix needed: cache-invalidation coverage already exists in peony/tests/link_and_run.rs (incremental_reuse_and_relink) and peony/tests/more_cases.rs (incremental_partial_change).
dismissed.reason: The candidate's central claim — that the invalidation/cache-miss path is untested because incremental_cache_full_reuse only covers the cache-HIT path — is factually wrong. Its evidence was scoped only to algorithms.rs and missed two existing tests in the same test crate. incremental_reuse_and_relink (link_and_run.rs:114) and incremental_partial_change (more_cases.rs:193) both link with --incremental, modify an input, relink, assert the cache is NOT reused, and assert the binary produces the new correct value. These tests would catch the exact over-eager-reuse / silent incremental miscompile regression the candidate describes. The claimed coverage gap does not exist, so the candidate is invalid.

dismissed.summary: Candidate's nondeterminism theory is incorrect for this codebase; fixture comment mismatch is real but trivial
dismissed.evidence: The candidate argues the fixture is too simple to expose work-stealing nondeterminism in write_section_data_parallel (peony-emit/src/lib.rs:296-366). However, the parallel section writes are intentionally disjoint by construction (QUAD Theorem 5.1 / Theorem 4.1): each input section occupies a unique, non-overlapping file range, so thread scheduling order cannot affect output bytes regardless of how many objects, GOT slots, or weak symbols are present. The test runs 8 independent linker invocations writing to 8 separate output files (det_0.out..det_7.out) and compares them — it tests cross-run determinism (e.g. nondeterministic symbol table ordering, ASLR-influenced layout), not intra-link thread-scheduling races. The fixture genuinely lacks .weak directives and GOT/PLT relocations, making the comment on line 124 ('multiple sections, relocations, weak symbols') inaccurate, but that is a doc-comment mismatch, not a meaningful gap in test coverage for the nondeterminism property being verified. Adding more objects or GOTPCREL relocations would not make latent ordering bugs more detectable because the disjoint-range guarantee eliminates them architecturally.
dismissed.fix: Correct the fixture comment on line 124 to accurately describe what the assembly produces: two sections (.data, .text), two global symbols (x, _start), and two RIP-relative PC32 relocations — no weak symbols, no GOT/PLT slots. If the intent is to also stress cross-run GOT ordering determinism, add a second fixture with a GOTPCREL relocation, but note this is an enhancement, not a correctness fix for the current claim.
dismissed.reason: The candidate's central technical claim — that the fixture is too simple to exercise the work-stealing nondeterminism it claims to cover — is wrong.
